segments.s169-g145: که here is an introductory/relativizing particle (roughly 'that' or 'for') opening the clause. تاج سر is a possessive compound 'crown (تاج) of the head (سر)' and شهریاران is the plural genitive 'of the kings'; توی is an archaic/poetic spelling/form corresponding to توئی ('you are') so the whole phrase means 'you are the crown on the heads of kings.' Word order is emphatic: predicate (تاج سر شهریاران) before the copular pronoun-form توی.
segments.s169-g146: The second که repeats the relativizing/introducing particle. گوید here functions as a present/future reporting verb 'would say / says' (poetic; classical Persian uses گوید for 'he says'). The following که introduces the reported content: پور شبانان توی — پور 'son/descendant' + شبانان 'shepherds' (plural); توی again is the archaic copular form 'thou art / you are.' The clause asks rhetorically 'who would say that you are the son of shepherds?'.
segments.s169-g147: شبان 'shepherd' is the subject; نیست is the Existential/negative 'is not / there is not.' از + گوهر تو literally 'from/of your essence' — a poetic way to say 'of your worth/quality.' کسی in this negative context functions as 'anyone / no one' (i.e. 'there is no shepherd of your caliber'). Word order places the negation after the subject for emphasis.
segments.s169-g148: وزین ('and weighty') modifies داستان ('tale/story'); here وزین means 'weighty, dignified' and داستان 'tale/legend.' هست is the copula 'is/are' and با من 'with me' indicates possession/keeping of such tales; بسی functions adverbially 'many / much / enough.' The phrase means 'and (such) weighty tales I have in plenty.'
segments.s169-g149: ز بهر (a poetic inversion of بهرِ) functions as 'for / on behalf of / for the sake of.' جوان 'young (man)' is the beneficiary; اسپ 'steed/horse' is the object; و links the next object. بالای here means 'high/lofty' or 'superior' and modifies an implied noun (understood as saddle/wardrobe accoutrement in context); خواست is the past-tense lexical verb 'he wanted/desired.' The sequence lists the gifts he desired for the youth: a horse and something lofty/high.
segments.s169-g150: همان 'that very / the same' picks out the specific garment; جامه 'garment/robe' is the direct object; خسروآرای is a compound epithet meaning literally 'Khosrow-adorning' or 'fit to adorn a king' (an attributive name for a splendid robe); خواست again is 'he desired.' The clause states he also desired that particular kingly robe.
segments.s169-g151: به ایوان 'to the iwan/portico' uses به as the directional preposition. خرامید is a past verb meaning 'he strode/pranced/entered proudly'; با او 'with him' marks accompaniment; به هم 'together' reinforces that they went as a pair. Word order places the place first (to the iwan), then the verb and companions.
segments.s169-g152: روانش is روان + possessive -ش 'his spirit/soul/mood'; ز بهر 'because of / on account of' introduces the reason; سیاوش is the proper name Siavash; دژم is an adjective 'sullen / grim / ill-pleased.' The clause means 'his spirit was sullen for/over Siavash' (i.e. he felt grim because of Siavash).
segments.s169-g65: This clause is an introductory reporting formula. بدو is a prepositional/pronominal phrase 'to him' placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت is the past verb 'said' (poetic word order places the object/recipient before the verb). من is the speaker-marking pronoun 'I'. زین نوآمد is a compressed demonstrative+adjective phrase 'from this newly-arrived one' (زین = 'from this'; نوآمد = 'newly arrived'); بسی is an adverb 'many/much' that anticipates and modifies the following noun phrase (سخن‌ها).
segments.s169-g66: سخن‌ها is the plural noun 'words/tales' (definite by context) and is the direct object of شنیدستم, an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'I heard' or 'I was told' with the speaker as subject. از هر کسی is a prepositional phrase 'from every person' (از = 'from', هر = 'every', کسی = 'person/one') specifying the source of those reports.
segments.s169-g67: Literal order is inverted for poetic effect: پر آشوب modifies روزگار ('a time full of turmoil'). پر here is the prepositional/adjectival element 'full (of)', آشوب 'turmoil', and جنگست is a fused form 'is war' or 'is battle' (copula + predicate). ز (poetic preposition 'from/of') + و (here part of an older locution linking to روزگار) together relate the predicate to 'that time' — overall: 'the times are full of tumult and war.'
segments.s169-g68: همه یاد دارم is a simple clause 'I remember all' (همه = 'all/everything', یاد = 'memory/remembrance', دارم = present 'I have / I hold' used here as 'I remember'). ز آموزگار is an ablative/instrumental 'from (my) teacher' (ز = 'from', آموزگار = 'teacher'), indicating the source of the recollection.
segments.s169-g69: که is the subordinating particle 'that' (introducing a reported prophecy/relative clause). از تخمه تور = 'from the lineage/stock of Tur' (از = 'from', تخمه = 'offspring/seed', تور = proper name Tūr). وز کیقباد is a poetic juxtaposition 'and/from Kay Qobad' where وز is an archaic connective/particle (here linking the two lineages) and کیقباد is the proper name 'Kay Qobad'.
segments.s169-g70: یکی شاه 'one king' (یکی = 'one/a certain', شاه = 'king'). سر برزند is a poetic verbal phrase meaning 'will raise his head' or more idiomatically 'will arise/emerge' (سر = 'head'; برزند is an older poetic form indicating upward/emergent action). بانژاد is an adjective/adverbial 'of noble birth / by lineage' qualifying the king.
segments.s169-g71: This is a nominal-predicative construction: جهان را is the affected topic 'the world' (را marks it as definite/affected), به مهر وی آید is 'a love/affection for him comes' (به مهر = 'with/by love' or 'to love', وی = 'him', آید = present/future sense 'comes/arrives'). نیاز here is a noun 'longing/affection' that functions as the thing that comes upon the world.
segments.s169-g72: همه شهر توران is the subject phrase 'all the cities of Turan' (همه = 'all', شهر = 'cities', توران = proper name). برندش is a verbal form 'they bring/take to him' or 'they offer him' (poetic present plural), where the clitic -ش marks 'him' as the object; نماز is the object 'prayer(s)' meaning ritual prayer or devotions that the cities offer.
segments.s169-g25: This clause narrates the action in a compact, archaic poetic form: یکی (literally 'one' used as 'someone') is the subject, followed by اندرآی (an older/poetic form meaning 'entered' or 'came in'). The conjunction و links to شگفتی ببین, where ببین is the bare present/subjunctive/optative form used in classical Persian narrative to mean 'saw' or 'beheld' (the final -د is often dropped for meter). Word order is Subject – Verb – (conjoined) Verb/Predicate.
segments.s169-g26: This noun phrase names what was seen: بزرگی 'greatness' and رای 'counsel/plan' of جهان‌آفرین ('World‑Creator'). جهان‌آفرین is a compound noun modifying رای (genitive relation), and بزرگی is coordinated with رای by و. Classical Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe here; the structure is effectively 'the greatness and the counsel of the Creator of the world.'
segments.s169-g27: تو گویی is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic phrase meaning 'you would say' or 'one might say' (تو here functions like a rhetorical/impersonal subject). نشاید is an impersonal negative — 'it is not fitting/appropriate' — and مگر introduces the exception 'except/only'. تاج را has the object marker را attached, marking 'the crown' as the definite item singled out by the exception.
segments.s169-g28: و‌گر is a conditional/contrasting particle ('or if' / 'but if'), starting an alternative: a series of nouns (جوشن 'cuirass/armor', ترگ 'rag/shred', تاراج 'plunder') joined by و. The final را again marks the whole coordinated list as the definite object/exception. The coordination and را show these items are being presented as the alternative fitting things.
segments.s169-g29: Syntactically simple: سپهبد (the spahbed, a title) is the subject and بیامد is the past-tense verb 'came'. بر شهریار uses the preposition بر (classical use meaning 'to/before' someone) plus شهریار 'king' to mark direction/recipient ('came to the king' / 'came before the king').
segments.s169-g30: بسی is an adverbial intensifier here ('much' / 'greatly'), followed by آفرین کرد, a light-verb construction where آفرین ('acclamation/praise') + کرد ('did/made') = 'uttered much praise'. The coordination و links the next clause: بردش is a clitic-bearing verb form (برد + ـش 'him/it') and نثار functions as a noun/adverb meaning 'as an offering' or 'in homage' — together giving 'and carried/presented him/it as an offering'.
segments.s169-g31: This is a stacked locative/nominal description: بران ('on/thereon' or 'upon that') introduces locations, followed by coordinated adjectives/nouns برز و بالا ('height/high/upwards') and then آن شاخ و یال ('that horn and mane'). The coordination strings several modifiers/parts together to paint a visual scene; there is no ezāfe but the nouns are linked by و as is normal in classical verse.
segments.s169-g32: تو گویی again functions idiomatically ('you would say' / 'one might say'). برو here is the verbal root 'go' (used in the sense 'has gone' with the following compound), and برگذشته‌ست is a perfective/periphrastic expression meaning 'has passed over' or 'has elapsed' — together with سال 'year' yielding 'you would say a year has passed.' The verb phrase is in the perfective narrative register typical of classical Persian.
segments.s169-g209: بیامد is the simple past 'he came'. به marks direction 'to'. درگاه is a formal noun 'threshold/royal court' (here 'court, presence of the king'), and افراسیاب is the proper name of the king; the phrase = 'he came to Afrasiab's court'. Word order is subject-verb (implicit subject) + prepositional phrase.
segments.s169-g210: This is a compressed adjectival/participial description of the same person. جهانی functions as an adjective 'worldly' or 'of the world'. برو here is poetic/elliptical and attached to جهانی to give a sense like 'worldly‑looking' or 'one who goes in the world' (an epithet rather than the literal imperative). دیده (literally 'eye') + کرده (perfect participle 'made/caused') form a small clause 'having made (his) eyes' and پرآب describes the state 'full of water' (i.e. tearful). Together: 'worldly in appearance, his eyes made full of water.'
segments.s169-g211: روارو is an adverbial/poetic phrase 'face‑to‑face' / 'stepping forward'. برآمد is past 'arose/stepped forth'. که introduces a subordinate clause or quoted instruction 'that/so (do)'. بگشای is an imperative 'open' (prefix بـ plus present stem گشای), and راه is the object 'the way' or 'path' (here 'the way into the court'). The segment expresses the act of advancing and calling to open the way.
segments.s169-g212: The second که begins the explanatory clause 'for/that'. آمد is past 'has come'. نوآیین is an adjective 'new, newly arrived'. یکی here means 'one' / 'someone' (a certain person). پیشگاه means 'the royal presence / court' ('in the sight of the king'). The clause explains why the way should be opened: 'because a newcomer has come to the court.'
segments.s169-g213: همی is a customary poetic particle indicating continuous or ongoing action ('was/kept on'). رفت is past 'went'. پیش اندرون شاه is a prepositional phrase 'forward into the king's inner quarters' (پیش = 'forward/toward', اندرون = 'inner part/inner court', شاه = 'the king'). گرد here has the sense 'about/around' and helps indicate movement into the king's immediate circle. Overall: 'he kept going forward into the king's inner court.'
segments.s169-g214: سپهدار is a title 'commander' or high military officer. پیران here is the proper name 'Piran' (a character), so it functions as the name after the title. ورا = 'to him' / 'towards him' (object/goal). پیش برد is a verbal compound 'brought forward / advanced before (the king)' — بردن = 'to bring/carry' in past; in this line it means the commander escorted or presented him forward into the king's presence.
segments.s169-g215: بیامد is past 'he came'. به marks direction 'to/near'. نزدیک = 'near/close'. افراسیاب is the king's name. The segment simply states 'he came close to Afrasiab.'
segments.s169-g216: This clause describes a bodily reaction. نیا is a noun here (literally 'ancestor' or 'the old one') used as the person whose face is described. را is the object/definiteness marker used in an older construction to link the possessor to the possessed (نیا را رخ = 'Niyâ's face'). رخ = 'face'. از + شرم = 'from/because of shame'. او = 'him' (reference to the approaching person). شد = 'became'. پرآب = 'full of water' i.e. 'moist/tearful.' Word order is possessive-like: 'Niyâ's face, from shame at him, became tearful.'
segments.s169-g265: Subject phrase 'فرنگیس و کیخسرو' (two proper names) precedes the adverb 'آنجا' and the past verb 'رسید'. In classical/poetic Persian the simple past 'رسید' here carries the meaning 'arrived/reached' for the compound subject; word order is subject — place — verb.
segments.s169-g266: 'بسی مردم آمد' = 'many people came' with 'بسی' functioning as an adverb/quantifier. The following phrase 'ز هرسو پدید' (‘from every side, appeared’) completes the action: 'آمد' (came) and 'پدید' (appeared/emerged) form a vivid two‑verb sequence, the preposition 'ز' (from) introduces the source 'هرسو' (everywhere).
segments.s169-g267: 'بدیده' (ill‑fated/ill‑favoured) functions as an adjectival/nominal phrase referring to the recipients (poetic ellipsis omits an explicit preposition). The verb 'سپردند' is 3rd person plural past 'they entrusted/handed over'; 'یک‌یک زمین' ('the land one by one') is the object, meaning 'each parcel of land' was handed over.
segments.s169-g268: This is a compound noun phrase functioning as the indirect object of 'سپردند' (without an overt preposition, a common poetic usage). 'زبان دد' = 'the enemy’s tongue/speech' (زبان in the sense 'speech'); 'و دام پرآفرین' = 'and the snare full of praise' i.e. a flattering trap. The two nouns are linked by 'و' and together indicate to whom/into what hands the land was given.
segments.s169-g269: 'همی گفت' is a habitual/continuous past expression ('would say' / 'kept saying'); 'هرکس که بودش هنر' is a relative clause 'whoever had (بودش) virtue/skill (هنر)'. The enclitic 'ش' on 'بود' refers back to 'هرکس' and conveys possession ('he had').
segments.s169-g270: An exclamatory/nominal phrase: 'سپاس' (praise/thanks) governed by 'از' = 'of/about/to' pointing to the addressee/recipient 'جهان‌داور' (the world‑ordainer/judge), with 'دادگر' as an epithet meaning 'just'. Together: 'Praise to the just world‑ordainer.'
segments.s169-g271: 'کزان' is a relativizer/adverb meaning 'from which' and introduces the phrase that follows. 'بیخ برکنده فرخ درخت' = 'the uprooted root of the fortunate tree': 'بیخ' (root) modified by the past participle 'برکنده' (uprooted), followed by 'فرخ درخت' ('fortunate tree') in genitive relationship; overall this is the source/topic for the next clause.
segments.s169-g272: 'ازین‌گونه شاخی' = 'a branch of this kind' (ازین‌گونه = 'of this sort'); the verb 'برآورد' here is past‑tense 'brought forth/produced' agreeing with the singular tree subject, and 'سخت' is an adverbial intensifier 'strongly/with force'. The clause thus says that such a branch was brought forth vigorously.
segments.s169-g89: جهان‌آفرین را نیایش گرفت is a compact past‑tense clause meaning 'he prayed to the Creator.' جهان‌آفرین (placed before the verb) is the definite object, marked by را; نیایش گرفت is a light‑verb construction (نیایش + گرفت) where گرفت (past, 3rd sg.) means 'took up/held' and idiomatically 'offered prayer.' The object‑marker را confirms that جهان‌آفرین is the target of the prayer.
segments.s169-g90: به شاه جهان بر ستایش گرفت continues the action: گرفت (same past 3rd sg.) governs the phrase 'took up praise.' به + شاه جهان marks the indirect target 'to/for the King of the World' (شاه جهان is a compound title). بر ستایش here means 'toward praise' or 'as regards praise' (بر used in the sense 'toward/with respect to'), so the whole phrase = 'he directed his praise to the King of the World.'
segments.s169-g91: پراندیشه بد تا به ایوان رسید is a temporal clause: پراندیشه بد ('deeply worried/careworn') describes his state; تا introduces the time boundary 'until/unto,' and به ایوان رسید is the verb phrase 'reached the iwan/portal' (رسید, past 3rd sg.). Word order places the descriptive phrase before the temporal clause and verb, as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s169-g92: کزان رنج و مهرش چه آید پدید is a dependent clause introduced by کزان ('from which' / 'from that'), referring back to his arrival. The coordinated direct objects رنج و مهرش ('suffering and his/her love', مهر + enclitic -ش) are the things whose appearance is questioned; چه آید پدید = 'what will come forth / appear' (آید is present/future‑oriented use of آمد in a poetic register, and پدید is a light verb-like predicate 'appear, emerge').
segments.s169-g93: شبانان کوه قلا را بخواند is a past clause where بخواند (past 3rd sg. of خواندن) means 'he called/summoned.' کوه قلا is a genitive compound 'mountain‑fort/fortress on the mountain' modifying شبانان. را marks the direct object 'the youths/shepherds of the mountain‑fort' as what he summoned.
segments.s169-g94: وزان خرد چندی سخن‌ها براند: وزان ('and from that') ties the following to the previous action; خرد ('wisdom') is the source, چندی functions adverbially 'for a while/a few (moments),' and سخن‌ها (the direct object) precedes the verb براند. براند (past 3rd sg. of راندن used poetically) means 'sent forth / brought out' — here 'uttered' or 'set forth words.'
segments.s169-g95: که این را بدارید چون جان پاک is an instructional clause: بدارید is an imperative/jussive form (2nd pl.) of داشتن meaning 'keep/hold/consider,' so 'keep this as/like a pure soul.' که introduces the clause, این را marks the object 'this,' and چون + جان پاک is a simile/comparative 'as a pure soul.'
segments.s169-g96: نباید که بیند ورا باد و خاک is a negative impersonal clause: نباید ('it must not / it should not be') introduces the subordinate clause که + ...; بیند is present/subjunctive of دیدن 'to see,' ورا is the enclitic object 'him/it,' and باد و خاک ('wind and dust') are the logical subject placed after the verb for poetic word order. The sense is 'it should not be that wind and dust see him.'
segments.s169-g225: بپرسید is a past-tense 3rd-person plural verb here ("they asked"); it introduces direct speech. کای is an archaic vocative particle meaning "O," used to address someone. نورسیده (literally "newly-born/young") is a participial adjective meaning "inexperienced/tender," modifying جوان, which here means "young man/ youth." The whole phrase functions as the clause "they asked, 'O inexperienced youth...'"
segments.s169-g226: چه is the interrogative "what/how." آگاه is an adjective/noun meaning "aware/knowing (one)"; داری is 2nd-person singular present of داشتن "to have" ("you have"). ز is the poetic preposition "of/from," and کار جهان is a genitive phrase "the affairs/workings of the world." Together the clause asks "what knowledge do you have of the world's affairs?" — note the SOV tendency where the verb (داری) appears after the question word and predicate.
segments.s169-g227: بر is the preposition "on/upon/over" governing گوسفندان (the sheep, here the flock). چه گردی همی is an archaic interrogative construction: چه = "how/what," گردی is a verbal-noun/derived form meaning "wandering/roaming," and همی is an old-period particle adding continuity/emphasis (roughly like an archaic progressive). Together: "How (in what way) do you roam about the sheep?"
segments.s169-g228: زمین را is object/topic-marked "the land/earth." چه گونه is the formulaic interrogative "in what way/how," سپردی is the 2nd-person past of سپردن "to entrust/hand over," and همی again is the archaic particle (emphatic/continuative). The clause literally asks "How did you entrust the land?" (i.e. in what manner have you left the land to be governed).
segments.s169-g229: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed word order meaning "thus gave answer" (چنین = thus/so, داد = gave (past)), followed by a complementizer که introducing the reported content. نخچیر means "prey/game," and نیست is the copula-negation "is not/there is not." So this segment reports: "He gave this answer: that there is no game (for me)."
segments.s169-g230: مرا ... نیست is an idiomatic possession/availability construction meaning "there is not to me / I do not have ..." (مرا = to me; note the segment's tokenization shows 'م' + 'را'). خود here intensifies/means "my own / myself." کمان و پر تیر is a coordination "bow and (quiver) of arrows" (پر = feathers, used with تیر to indicate a quiver/arrow-stock). نیست negates the whole: "I myself have no bow nor (quiver of) arrows."
segments.s169-g231: بپرسید is again the past 3rd-person plural "they asked." بازش here combines باز "again" with the enclitic -ش (him), so the phrase means "they asked him again." ز introduces the source/agent "from the teacher" (آموزگار). Note the enclitic pronoun is attached to باز rather than as a separate word.
segments.s169-g232: This is a prepositional/genitive list introduced by ز "about/from." نیک و بد = "the good and the bad," followed by و گردش روزگار "and the turning/circuit of time/fate" (گردش = turn/vicissitude, روزگار = time/fate). The whole phrase asks about moral quality and the vicissitudes of fortune.
segments.s169-g177: تو خود is an emphatic subject: تو (you) + خود (self) doubles the emphasis ('you yourself'). این is a demonstrative referring to the foregoing idea; میندیش is the imperative 'think' (classical spelling, present imperative). و links the clauses. بد را is the object phrase with را marking the definite/targeted object ('evil' or 'badness'), and مکوش is a classical negative imperative (م‑ prefix + کوش 'strive') meaning 'do not endeavor' or 'do not pursue.'
segments.s169-g178: چه at the front is an interrogative 'what'; گفت here is the verb 'said' (past) introducing reported speech — note the provided sense list lacks the 'say' sense so the index is -1. آن is the demonstrative 'that' qualifying خردمند 'wise man'; بسیارهوش is a compound adjective 'very clever/very wise' modifying خردمند and comes after the noun in the poetic order.
segments.s169-g179: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the reported clause. پروردگار is the subject 'the Lord / Nurturer.' از marks the comparative 'than' (literally 'from'); پدر is the standard of comparison 'father.' برترست is the predicate 'is superior' in a compact poetic form (برتر + است contracted).
segments.s169-g180: اگر is the conditional 'if.' زاده را here means 'for the child / the child's' with را marking the target of مهر: 'زاده را مهر' = 'affection for the child.' مهر is 'affection / love.' با introduces the locus/holder of that affection ('with'); مادرست is a contracted poetic form (مادر + است) meaning 'is with the mother' or simply 'belongs to the mother.' The overall sense: 'if the child's love is with the mother.'
segments.s169-g181: نخستین is an adverb 'first' or 'first of all.' به پیمان is an instrumental phrase 'by/with the covenant' (به + noun). م (here part of مرا as split in the tokenization) + را correspond to the object 'me' (مرا) with را marking it; because the token list splits م from را, the provided sense for م does not match normal pronoun usage (so index -1). شاد is the adjective 'happy/cheerful' and کن is the imperative 'do / make' (شاد کن = 'make (me) glad').
segments.s169-g182: ز is the poetic variant of از 'from/by.' سوگند شاهان is a genitive construction 'the oaths of the kings.' یکی is 'one (of them)' and یاد کن is an imperative 'remember / mention / recount' (یاد کردن can mean 'to mention' in narrative contexts). The phrase means 'from the kings' oaths, mention one.'
segments.s169-g183: فریدون is the proper name 'Fereydun.' The repeated به before each noun is a poetic listing marker here: به داد و به تخت و کلاه = 'in/with justice, and with the throne, and (with) the crown.' داد means 'justice' (not the verb 'gave' in this context), تخت 'throne,' and کلاه 'hat/crown' (here 'crown').
segments.s169-g184: همی is a frequentative/adverb 'always, continually.' داشتی is a classical past/imperfect form of داشتن meaning here 'would have/kept' — in classical poetry the -ی ending appears where modern Persian might use داشت. راستی را is 'the truth' with را marking it as definite object. نگاه is the verbal noun / stem related to نگاه داشتن 'to keep/hold'; the phrase (راستی را نگاه + داشتی) is a poetic inversion meaning 'he kept (held) the truth.'
segments.s169-g249: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' marking the source; a list of nouns follows (اسپ 'horse' and پرستنده 'attendant/follower') joined by و. بیش و کم is a fixed paired phrase ('more and less', 'many or few') that qualifies number/degree of the preceding nouns; word order is a simple coordinated nominal phrase.
segments.s169-g250: بده is an imperative 'give' addressing someone; هرچ ('whatever') is the free relative acting as the direct object; باید here functions as 'is necessary' or 'required' describing هرچ (i.e. 'whatever is needed'); ز گنج و درم is a prepositional source phrase 'from the treasure and the coin' modifying the action of giving.
segments.s169-g251: سپهبد is the subject. برو کرد is an older/poetic periphrasis where برو ('go') + کرد (past of 'do') yields the meaning 'went off/caused to go' (a verbal compound); لختی 'for a short while' is an adverbial modifier and شتاب 'haste' functions as an adverbial/noun describing manner — overall this is a compact, slightly inverted clause meaning 'the spahbed made haste (and went) for a short while'.
segments.s169-g252: برون is an adverb 'out/forth' paired with the verbal action; بردش is the verb plus enclitic object '‑ش' = 'him' (literally 'carried/took him'), and از پیش افراسیاب is a prepositional phrase 'from before/in front of Afrasiab' specifying source/location. Together: '(he) brought/took him out from before Afrasiab.'
segments.s169-g253: به ایوان خویش is the prepositional phrase 'to/into his iwan'; خویش is the possessive reflexive 'his/own' attached after ایوان. آمد is the past-tense verb 'came'. افروخته is a postverbal adjective/participle meaning 'aflame/angry/illuminated' describing his state as he arrived—poetic placement of the adjective after the verb is common.
segments.s169-g254: خرامان is an adverb/verb-form meaning 'with a stately/proud gait' (describing manner). و simply links the next phrase. چشم بدی دوخته literally has 'چشم' (eye) + modifier بدی ('evil/bad') + دوخته (past participle of دوختن 'to stitch/fix'); the phrase functions idiomatically as 'with eyes fixed in an evil/hostile gaze' — a verbless descriptive unit where دوخته describes the state of the eyes.
segments.s169-g255: همی گفت is a durative construction: همی before a past verb marks continuous or repeated action ('he kept saying'). کز is the conjunction 'that/which' introducing the reported clause. دادگر کردگار is a nominal phrase in apposition/genitive: دادگر 'just' qualifying کردگار 'the Creator/God' (i.e. 'the Just Creator').
segments.s169-g256: درخت نو is the subject 'a new tree'. آمد is the past verb 'came' used here in the idiom آمد ... به بار ('came to bearing') meaning 'has borne fruit' or 'has begun to bear'. جهان را is the definite object marked by را ('for the world' / 'the world is affected'). به بار is a verbal complement meaning 'into fruit/fruitfulness' — together: 'a new tree has come to bear fruit for the world.'
segments.s169-g161: کز is an old/conjunctive form introducing a cause clause (“because/that”); اندیشه بد = “bad thought(s)” in noun-adjective order (noun before adjective is normal in Persian poetry); همه شب is an adverbial phrase “the whole night”; دلم is دل + -م (possessive enclitic “my”), so the segment sets up “Because of bad thoughts, all night my heart …” and the verb follows in the next segment.
segments.s169-g162: بپیچید is a past tense verb (perfective) meaning “twisted/writhed” (third‑person singular here, agreeing with دلم as a topicalized subject-object order common in poetry); وز here functions causatively/ablative “from/because of” (archaic use) and غم = “grief”; همی is the old continuous/iterative prefix/adverbial “continually/kept on”; بگسلم is a verbal form of گسستن “to break/part” (first or third person sense determined by syntax), so the clause says the heart writhed and was repeatedly torn apart by sorrow.
segments.s169-g163: ازین (از + این) means “from this/that”; کودکی is a noun “childhood” or “youth” and here refers to the earlier years/origin; کز introduces a relative clause “which/that”; سیاوش is the proper name Siavash; رسید is past tense “arrived/came/was received,” so the whole segment means “from this childhood that came from Siavash.” Word order is typical for classical Persian: locative/temporal phrase + relative clause.
segments.s169-g164: تو گفتی is the past 2nd‑person “you said/told”; مرا is the object “to me” (here split in the word list as م + را — the enclitic object + object marker) and marks the indirect object “you told me”; روز شد ناپدید is a complete clause “the day became vanished” with شد as the past of شدن “became” and ناپدید “vanished/gone.” In poetry the report clause follows immediately after “you said to me.”
segments.s169-g165: نبیره is “offspring/descendant/scion” (here: descendant of Fereydun); فریدون is the name Fereydun; شبان is “shepherd(s)” and acts as the milieu in which he was raised; پرورد is a past/passive participial or verbal form of پروردن “to rear/nurture,” so the phrase says he is a descendant of Fereydun who was reared as/among shepherds. Adjective/agent phrases follow the noun in this poetic ordering.
segments.s169-g166: ز رای و خرد = “from counsel and wisdom” (ز = poetic genitive/ablative “from/of”); این = “this/the foregoing”; کی is an interrogative here meaning “who” (classical Persian often uses کی for “who” rather than when); اندر خورد is a compound where خورد (from خوردن in the middle voice) means “befall/happen to” (not literal “eat”), so the question asks how such a thing could occur to someone characterized by counsel and wisdom.
segments.s169-g167: ازوگر is an archaic conditional “if from him / if it be that”; نوشته can be read as the past participle “written” or “writing” (here: something written to me); به من = “to me”; بر بدی‌ست literally “it is toward/beside badness” — idiomatically, “it concerns a wrong done to me.” The clause sets a conditional premise (“if a note written to me alleges a wrong…”).
segments.s169-g168: نشاید is an impersonal present/past modal “it is not fitting/ought not to be” (classical jussive/optative sense); گذشتن here is used in the sense “to pass over/forgive/let go” (not merely spatial “to pass”); که introduces the reason clause “for/that”; آن ایزدی‌ست = “that is divine/heavenly” (ایزدی = of the divine), so the sentence says such a thing ought not be overlooked because it is (a matter of) the divine.
segments.s169-g57: This is an imperative clause. از + noun phrase (از اندیشه بد) is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of (evil) thought' placed before the verb for emphasis; بپرداز is an imperative verb (poetic/archaic form of پرداختن here used to mean 'cast off / rid') and دل is either the direct object 'cast off from the heart' or a vocative 'O heart' addressed by the speaker.
segments.s169-g58: Two coordinated imperatives: برافراز (raise/exalt) governs تاج (the direct object 'crown'). The conjunction و links a parallel, elliptic clause where the verb is understood again before برفراز دل ('and [raise] loftily/uplift the heart'); برفراز here is an adverbial/verb-prefix meaning 'on high' or 'loftily'.
segments.s169-g59: چنان is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the past verb کرد ('made/caused'). روشن is the adjective/complement 'bright/clear'; جهان‌آفرین (the world‑creator) is the agent/subject, placed after the verb for poetic inversion (normal prose order would be جهان‌آفرین چنان کرد روشن). کرد is simple past 3rd person.
segments.s169-g60: کزو is a relative/compound pronoun 'from which / from whom' referring back to that brightness; دور شد is a past tense passive/inchoative 'became distant / was driven away.' The list جنگ و بیداد و کین are coordinated nouns (war, injustice/oppression, and hatred/vengeance) that became removed as a result.
segments.s169-g61: روانش is روان + possessive suffix -ش 'his/its soul' and functions as the subject. ز introduces the cause 'from/because of' (ز خون سیاوش = 'because of Siavash's blood'). به درد is a prepositional/causal phrase 'into pain/sorrow' that prepares the complement for the verb in the next segment.
segments.s169-g62: برآورد is a past tense verb here 'brought/caused' (verb precedes its logical subject for poetic effect). بر لب is a locative phrase 'upon (the) lip(s)'. یکی باد سرد is an indefinite noun phrase 'one/a certain cold wind' (یکی = 'one/a certain' + باد 'wind' + سرد 'cold'). Together: 'he caused / there came upon his lips a cold wind.'
segments.s169-g63: پشیمان بشد is a predicative construction meaning 'became regretful' (بشد = 'became' / past event). زان (from that / because of that) introduces the cause; کجا here functions in an archaic/poetic sense as a relative/indefinite 'that which/what' (i.e. 'for what he had done'). کرده بود is the past perfect 'had done', so the whole clause means 'he became regretful for what he had done.'
segments.s169-g64: به گفتار بیهوده is an ablative/cause phrase 'by/through idle/vain speech' (به = 'by/with' here). آزرده بود is past progressive/state 'was distressed' or 'had been troubled' (بود = past 'was'). The clause states the prior state that caused his regret: he had been vexed by pointless talk.
segments.s169-g233: بدو (= be + ضمیر) is the dative/clitic meaning “to him/unto it.” گفت is the past 'said' (here functioning as the speech verb) followed by the content clause. جایی که باشد is a relative/subordinate clause ‘a place where there may be/is’ with باشد in the present/subjunctive sense ‘be/is.’ پلنگ is the noun head of that clause. Word order places the verb/auxiliary (باشد) before the noun it licenses, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s169-g234: بدرد is used predicatively here meaning ‘to be of use/benefit’; it governs دل مردم (‘the hearts of the people’) as the indirect object. تیزچنگ appears at the end as the nominative/theme — either an epithet or a name — so the clause reads ‘Tizchang is of benefit to the people's hearts.’ The clause order places predicate before subject, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s169-g235: سه دیگر = ‘three more’ (i.e., three additional items/questions). بپرسیدش is the imperative/optative stem meaning ‘ask him’ (بپرسید + object clitic -ش). از introduces the topic/source of the questions (‘about/concerning’), and مام و باب = ‘mother and father’ (colloquial pair), so the whole segment commands: ‘ask him three more about mother and father.’
segments.s169-g236: This line lists further topics introduced by the prepositions ز/از = ‘from/about.’ ز ایوان و از شهر = ‘about the iwan (porch) and about the city.’ وز here is a fused form (poetic و + از) meaning ‘and from/about’; خورد و خواب = ‘eating and sleeping’ (i.e., sustenance and rest). The repeated prepositions are a poetic listing device.
segments.s169-g237: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase ‘thus gave answer’ (چنین = thus; داد = gave; پاسخ = answer). The conjunction که introduces the content clause: درنده شیر = ‘a ferocious/predatory lion’ (درنده qualifying شیر). The noun follows its adjective in this order typical of Persian poetic syntax.
segments.s169-g238: نیارد here functions as a negative verb meaning ‘does not/will not bring’ or ‘will not cause to be’ (an archaic/poetic form corresponding to نیاورد); because the head-sense list lacks this sense it is marked -1. سگ کارزاری = ‘a campaign/war dog’ (سگ modified by کارزاری). به زیر = ‘under/underneath’ (پرهیز: ‘to bring under’). Together: ‘a ferocious lion will not be brought under by a war-dog.’
segments.s169-g239: بخندید خسرو = ‘Khosrow laughed.’ The preposition/particle ز = ‘from/because of’ marks the cause: ز گفتار اوی = ‘at his words’ or ‘because of his speech.’ اوی is the older/poetic form of the pronoun functioning possessively/obliquely (‘his’/‘him’), so the whole segment: ‘Khosrow laughed at his words.’
segments.s169-g240: سوی = ‘toward’; پهلوان = ‘champion/hero’; سپه = ‘army/host’; کرد + روی = a compound meaning ‘made/turned (his) face’ (کرد = do/make as light verb, روی = face). So the clause literally reads ‘he turned his face toward the army’s champion.’ The light verb construction (کرد + noun) is common in Persian.
segments.s169-g281: به دی‌مه is a temporal/prepositional phrase 'in/at the dusk' (به + دی‌مه). نشان بهاران is a noun + modifier: نشان (sign) modified by بهاران (of spring), i.e. 'the sign of spring'. بدی here is an archaic poetic 2nd-person past verb meaning 'you gave/revealed/made' (in classical verse the expected دادی is often metrically altered to بدی). Word order is topical: time phrase → object → verb.
segments.s169-g282: پرستشگه سوگواران is a possessed noun phrase: 'the place of worship / shrine of the mourners' (پرستشگه [shrine] with سوگواران in postnominal relation). بدی again functions as the 2nd-person past verb 'you made/gave'. The whole segment therefore means 'you made/gave the shrine of the mourners' or 'you set up a place of worship for the mourners.'
segments.s169-g283: چنین است is a copular/expletive fronting 'such/so it is'. کردار is the noun 'deed/conduct' and the following noun phrase این گنده پیر ('this large/great old man') is the possessor or topic: 'the conduct of this great old man' (in poetry the order and emphasis can invert). گنده acts as an intensifier/adjective before پیر.
segments.s169-g284: ستاند is a past tense verb 'he seized / he took' (classical/stylistic past). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. The noun phrase فرزند پستان شیر is a genitive chain: پستان شیر ('the lion's breast/teat') modified by فرزند (the offspring at that breast) — i.e. 'the suckling of the lion'. So the clause reads 'he took (it) from the lion's suckling.'
segments.s169-g285: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. پیوسته شد is a compound predicate 'became attached/continuous' (پیوسته functioning as adjective/participle and شد the past auxiliary 'became'). مهرِ دل is a genitive phrase 'the love/affection of the heart'. بر جهان gives the target 'upon the world' (بر marking the object of attachment).
segments.s169-g286: به خاک is directional 'to/toward the earth'. اندرآرد is a present/remote-classical-form verb 'brings / causes to go in' used transitively here with سرش ('his head') as its object: 'brings his head to the ground.' ناگهان is the adverb 'suddenly' placed after the verb for emphasis. The implied subject (he) carries over from the previous clause.
segments.s169-g287: تو is the topical subject 'you'. از وی 'from him' specifies the source. به جز شادمانی is a limiting phrase 'except for joy' (به جز = except). مجوی is the prohibitive jussive 'do not seek' (2nd person imperative/negative), so the full sense is 'You, seek from him nothing except joy.'
segments.s169-g288: به باغ جهان is a directional locative 'to the garden of the world' (به marking direction, باغ modified by جهان). برگ انده is a noun + past participle: برگ 'leaf' modified by انده 'placed/laid' (i.e. 'a leaf placed there'). مبوی is a negative imperative 'do not smell' (i.e. do not go to enjoy/seek its scent), a poetic prohibition. The line warns against seeking worldly delights.
segments.s169-g201: مرو is a negative imperative meaning “do not go/approach.” The phrase پیش او (preposition + pronoun) gives the location/target “before/in the presence of him.” جز + به + noun is an exception phrase meaning “except by/with …,” so جز به دیوانگی = “except in/by madness.” Word order is Verb – prepositional phrase – exception.
segments.s169-g202: مگردان is another negative imperative (do not turn/alter/do not make); زبان is the object meaning “speech/tongue.” جز به بیگانگی is again an exception phrase, here meaning “except into alienation/aloofness.” The clause thus prohibits changing one’s speech except into estrangement; the enclitic structure and verb morphology are poetic/concise.
segments.s169-g203: مگرد here functions as an admonitory negative (“do not become/act”), an archaic/poetic negative particle. ایچ‌گونه means “in this way/so” (here rendered as “thus”), and به گرد خرد literally “to/around wisdom” (گرد = around/about, خرد = wisdom), so the clause warns against behaving in this manner in relation to wisdom. Word order reflects older Persian: verb-particle then prepositional phrase.
segments.s169-g204: یک امروز is an adverbial phrase “one day/one today” (i.e. sometime/one day); بر تو is the dative/locative “upon you,” and بگذرد is the present-subjunctive/optative sense “may pass” or narrative subjunctive “pass.” مگر here introduces the possibility/rhetorical warning (“perhaps/else”), so the whole clause says “one day may pass over you.”
segments.s169-g205: به سر is a locative prepositional phrase “on/onto the head.” برنهادش is a past tense verb ‘placed on/raised up’ with the third‑person object suffix -ش (“him/it”). کلاه کیان is a possessive/genitive phrase “the cap/crown of the Kians.” Word order: locative phrase + verb(+clitic) + object phrase.
segments.s169-g206: ببستش is the past verb ‘he bound/tied’ with the object suffix -ش (“him”). کیانی qualifies the agent as ‘the Kayanian’ (royal house), کمر is the belt/girdle (here the thing bound), and بر میان means “around the middle/waist.” The clause describes fastening the girdle on his waist; verb+object enclitic is typical older Persian word order.
segments.s169-g207: یکی باره is a temporal phrase “one time/once” (یکی = one, باره = occasion). گام‌زن is a descriptive noun ‘a stepper/walker’ (here ‘fine‑striding man’), and خواست is the past-tense verb ‘wanted/wished.’ نغز is an adverb/adjective meaning “pithy/witty/delicate,” indicating the manner or quality of what he wished to do (to utter something witty).
segments.s169-g208: برو here is a narrative motion verb “he went” (literally “go”), followed by برنشست “he sat upon/ took a seat.” آن is the demonstrative “that/the” and گو is a formative element meaning “-speaking” or “one who says” (here forming the agentive epithet). پاک‌مغز is an epithet “pure‑minded.” Together آن گو پاک‌مغز = “that speaking pure‑minded (one),” i.e. “that pure‑minded speaker.”
segments.s169-g169: چو is a temporal conditional 'when'. کار گذشته is a noun phrase 'the matter/affair being past'. نیارد here functions as a verbal negative 'does not bring/come' (classical form corresponding to نیاورد), and به یاد is a prepositional phrase 'to mind/in memory' that completes the verb.
segments.s169-g170: زید شاد is an independent clause 'Zayd (be) glad' with شاد as a predicative adjective. و ما نیز باشیم شاد attaches another clause: باشیم is a 1st-person plural subjunctive/optative ('may we be' / 'let us be'), and نیز 'also' links the speaker(s) to Zayd.
segments.s169-g171: وگر is the conditional 'if'. هیچ here functions as an indefinite 'any/anyone' in a conditional clause. خوی بد is the noun phrase 'a bad disposition/trait'. آرد is a 3rd-person present classical verb 'brings/produces' and پدید 'forth/into view' modifies the verb (pědīd as 'appear').
segments.s169-g172: بسان 'like/as' introduces a comparison ('as with the father'). پدر modifies the following noun. سر is the direct object 'head'. بباید is the impersonal/modal form of باید (necessity/obligation: 'it is necessary / one must'), and برید here is the verbal base of بریدن 'to cut' (poetic word order places the object before the modal+verb), so the clause means 'the head must be cut'.
segments.s169-g173: بدو is the allative/adverbial 'to him' (to that person). گفت is a past-tense verb 'said' (the lemma list gives nominal senses, but here it is verbal past). پیران is the subject 'the elders'. که‌ای شهریار is the address: که‌ای (here functioning as the archaic/vocative 'O') + شهریار 'king'.
segments.s169-g174: ترا is split into the 2nd-person enclitic ت (w0) plus the object marker را (w1) and together mean 'you' as object ('to you'). خود adds emphasis 'yourself'. نباید is impersonal modal 'it is not necessary/you need not' or 'should not', and کس آموزگار is the noun phrase 'anyone as a teacher' (کس = anyone/someone, آموزگار = teacher), so the clause states that you yourself need no teacher.
segments.s169-g175: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain'. کودکی is a noun 'a child' (or 'childhood' depending on context) and خرد here usually means 'mind/wisdom' (but can also mean 'small'); together they describe 'a certain child of little/wiser mind'. چون in this position functions interrogatively 'how/when', and بیهشان is an archaic contracted form meaning 'without them' (بی + ایشان).
segments.s169-g176: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. کار گذشته is 'past deeds/the past matter'. چه دارد نشان؟ is a rhetorical question: چه 'what', دارد 3rd-person present 'has', نشان 'sign/token/trace' — 'what sign does he have (of the past)?'
segments.s169-g121: همی کرد is a habitual/continuous past construction (همی + simple past) meaning 'used to / kept on doing'. نخچیر آهو is a noun phrase 'nakhchīr-ye āhu' where نخچیر (prey/spoil) is governed by the following noun آهو (gazelle); نخست 'first/at first' marks temporal order.
segments.s169-g122: بر + شیر = 'against/upon the lion' (بر here = against/onto). و links the two coordinated objects/acts. جنگ پلنگان is 'battle (with) the leopards' where جنگ is 'combat' and پلنگان 'leopards'. نجست is used here in the sense 'to seek/pursue (a fight)' — it governs the coordinated phrase and completes the clause.
segments.s169-g123: کنون 'now' sets the time. نزد او = 'before/in the presence of him' (نزد = near/in front of). جنگ شیر دمان is a genitive/compound phrase 'the fight of/with the lion at the ḍamān' where دمان ('skirt/edge') qualifies the location or aspect of the lion-fight — overall functioning as the subject of the copula in the next segment.
segments.s169-g124: همانست = همان + است (copula) 'is the same'. The coordinating و joins the second clause: نخچیر آهو همان = 'the gazelle's spoil is likewise the same' — repetition of همان emphasizes unchanged state.
segments.s169-g125: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it must not / one should not'. که introduces the subordinate clause. آید is present/subjunctive of 'to come' used here in the sense 'that (it) come'. برو is the verb 'go' (here part of the verbal phrase indicating movement toward) and بر + گزند = 'upon harm' (گزند = harm), so the clause means 'it must not be that harm comes upon (him who goes)'.
segments.s169-g126: بیاویزدم is a first-person form (past/habitual or optative nuance) of 'to cling/attach oneself' (I would cling / I would hang on). پهلوان بلند is a noun phrase 'the tall champion' (پهلوان = hero/champion; بلند = tall/lofty) serving as the object/target of the verb.
segments.s169-g127: چو + بشنید = 'when (he/they) heard' (چو = when; بشنید = past 'heard'). پیران is the subject 'the elders'. بخندید و گفت = 'they laughed and said' — both verbs are past tense; گفت here functions as 'said' introducing the following content.
segments.s169-g128: نماند is past 'remained not / there remained no' (negative sense: nothing remains). نژاد و هنر = 'lineage and excellence/skill' as the coordinated nouns. در نهفت is a prepositional phrase 'hidden/inwardly kept' (در + نهفت), so the whole clause means 'no lineage or excellence remains hidden'.
segments.s169-g81: بگفت is a simple past 3rd-person verb 'he said'. آنچ (انچه) is a relative/demonstrative 'that which/whatever' and combines with یاد آمدش 'came to his memory' (یاد + آمد + enclitic -ش = 'came to him'). زین سخن literally 'from these words' links origin of the thought to the verb. Word order is verb → relative phrase → complement ('he said that which came to his mind from these words').
segments.s169-g82: همه نو is an adverbial phrase 'entirely/new in every respect' (همه modifying نو). شمرد is past 'he counted/considered'; its object is این سرای کهن 'this ancient sarā/verse' (این = 'this', سرای here functions as the poetic verse/chant or 'song/house' in context). The clause means 'he considered this old verse to be wholly new.'
segments.s169-g83: چه سازی is a rhetorical 'what can you do' (چه introducing a question/exclamation; سازی here expresses 'making/doing' in a rhetorical sense). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that': چاره بدست تو نیست 'the remedy/solution is not in your hand' — بدست is a locative 'in the hand/of the power of', with تو as the possessor; نیست negates existence/possession.
segments.s169-g84: درازست is a predicative adjective 'it is long/remote' (figuratively 'far-reaching' or 'out of reach'). در کام و شست تو نیست puts two loci in parallel: 'in the desire (کام) and in your thumb/fingertip (شست تو)' — idiomatically 'within your wish nor within your grasp' — and the clause ends with the negation نیست. The adjective and the locative phrases together mean 'it lies far from your desire and not within your reach.'
segments.s169-g85: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an adverbial/particle meaning 'thus/in this way'. بد بینی is a compound meaning 'seeing/considering as bad' where بد modifies بینی; بینی here is 2nd-person present 'you see' (classical present form). از روزگار 'of/from the course of the world (fate)' names the object of that ill-seeing.
segments.s169-g86: به نیکی is a locative/goal phrase 'toward goodness' (به + noun). همو 'that very one/the same' refers back to روزگار; باشد is a jussive/subjunctive 3rd-person 'may be' or 'let it be' and آموزگار is the predicate noun 'teacher'. The clause thus means 'let that very thing be a teacher toward goodness.'
segments.s169-g87: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. به در is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'to the court/presence' (به + در meaning 'to the royal presence/door'). پهلوان is the subject 'the champion/hero' and شادمان is a qualifying adjective 'joyful/rejoicing' describing him. Word order places the verb first, then the locative phrase and the nominal subject with its adjective.
segments.s169-g88: بدل here means 'recompense/return' (in return). بر همه is a prepositional phrase 'for/on everyone'. نیک is the predicate adjective 'good', and بودش is past 'was' with enclitic -ش ('it was to him/for him'), while گمان 'expectation/hope' names the mental state: 'his expectation was that the recompense for all would be good.' The whole sequence expresses his favorable expectation.
segments.s169-g289: This is a two-part conditional: اگر ... و‌گر ... = “if ... or if ...”. داری is the 2nd-person singular present of داشتن (have/possess). و‌گر is the archaic/poetic form of و اگر (or if). دست تنگ is an adjectival phrase meaning literally “hand tight” (i.e. short of means); adjective تنگ follows the noun here to form the idiom.
segments.s169-g290: نبینی is the negative 2nd-person form of دیدن used here in a jussive/prophetic sense — “you will not see.” همی is a classical aspectual particle marking continued or emphatic action. روزگار (fate/fortune/time) is the object; درنگ (a verbal noun) means “delay/tarry.” The clause literally says you will not see a delay of fortune (i.e. fortune will not stay).
segments.s169-g291: مرنجان is an imperative telling someone not to be upset (do not grieve). روان is vocative ‘soul’ or ‘spirit’ addressed directly. کاین is the contracted poetic form of که این (that this), introducing the subordinate clause. سرای ... نیست is a nominal clause with the copula نیست placed at the end: “this house/dwelling is not yours.”
segments.s169-g292: بجز is a preposition meaning “except.” تنگ تابوت is a noun phrase ‘narrow/close coffin’ (تنگ qualifies تابوت). جای تو نیست is an existential/categorical negation: ‘there is no place for you’ (جای = place; نیست = is not). The whole segment says only the cramped coffin remains as your place.
segments.s169-g293: نهادن is the infinitive ‘to put/set (aside)’ used rhetorically in the question نهادن چه باید؟ = ‘what need is there to put (aside)?’ چه باید is a rhetorical question (‘what should/what is necessary’). بخوردن is an infinitive ‘to eat’ placed before the imperative نشین (‘sit’) — a terse purposive/imperative construction meaning ‘sit (down) and eat’ or ‘sit to eat.’
segments.s169-g294: بر امید is a prepositional phrase ‘in/on the hope of.’ گنج جهان‌آفرین is a genitive/attributive phrase: گنج (treasure) qualified by جهان‌آفرین (‘world‑creator’ i.e. the Creator), so ‘in hope of the Creator’s treasure.’ Word order is noun + modifier in this compound.
segments.s169-g295: چو آمد is a temporal clause (‘when he/it came’); چو here = ‘when.’ به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase ‘to/near.’ سر is the direct object ‘head’ and تیغ شست is a verb phrase where شست is the past of شستن ‘washed’ used metaphorically of the sword (i.e. struck or cut). Classical/poetic word order permits the object (سر) and instrument (تیغ) to appear before the verb شست.
segments.s169-g296: مده is a negative imperative ‘do not give’ (here directed at wine). می‌که (می/می‌ + که) denotes wine (classical می = wine); the following که introduces the explanatory clause ‘for/because.’ از سال شد is a temporal/durational phrase ‘in the course of a year’ or ‘within a year’ and شد مرد مست = ‘the man became drunk.’ The clause gives the reason: do not give him wine, for within a year he became intoxicated.
segments.s169-g1: شبی قیرگون is an adverbial time phrase: شبی (an indefinite 'one night') modified by the adjective قیرگون ('pitch-dark') placed after it in poetic word-order. ماه پنهان‌شده is a noun (ماه, 'the moon') followed by a past-participle adjective (پنهان‌شده, 'hidden') — the participle describes the moon's state and implies 'the moon having been hidden'.
segments.s169-g2: به خواب اندرون is an adverbial phrase meaning 'into sleep/within sleep' (به + خواب + اندرون), where the preposition به marks direction/state. The enumerated nouns مرغ و دام و دده follow as the internal subjects that fall asleep; note the main verb ('fell asleep') is not overtly expressed here and is understood from context — a common poetic omission.
segments.s169-g3: چنان ('thus/so') opens the clause; دید is a simple past 'saw' and is the finite verb. سالار پیران is the subject ('the leader/commander of the elders'). به خواب ('in a dream') is an adjunct of manner/place (به + خواب). The whole phrase means 'Thus the commander of the elders saw in a dream...'.
segments.s169-g4: که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that...'. شمعی is an indefinite object ('a candle'); برافروختی is a past tense second-person form 'you kindled' (used here in narrative/vision address). ز marks source ('from/of') and آفتاب is the source noun 'the sun'. Word order is object + verb + source, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s169-g5: سیاوش is the vocative/subject. بر شمع is a prepositional phrase 'by/over the candle' (بر marks position). تیغی به دست is an instrumental/location phrase 'a sword in (his) hand'—تیغی indicates the presence of a sword and به دست ('in hand') specifies that it is held. The whole segment sketches Siyâvash's posture and setting before the speech.
segments.s169-g6: به آواز گفتی literally 'you spoke with a voice' i.e. 'you said aloud' — به marks manner ('in/with'), آواز = 'voice', and گفتی is the 2nd-person past 'you said' (here reported speech). The quoted clause نشاید نشست is an impersonal/modal statement: نشاید ('it is not fitting/ought not') followed by نشست ('to sit/remain seated') forming 'it is not proper to sit'.
segments.s169-g7: کزین ('from this') introduces source/starting-point. خواب نوشین is a compound noun phrase 'sweet/pleasant sleep' (خواب + adjective نوشین). سر آزاد کن is an imperative: سر ('head' or 'self') + آزاد ('free'/'liberate') + کن (imperative 'do'), i.e. 'free/raise your head' or 'rouse yourself' — a command addressed to the addressee.
segments.s169-g8: ز فرجام گیتی is a genitive phrase 'concerning/from the end of the world' (ز = 'about/from', فرجام = 'end/outcome', گیتی = 'the world'). یکی ('one' / 'one thing') is the object to be remembered, and یاد کن is an imperative 'remember' formed by the noun یاد ('remembrance') plus کن ('do!') — 'remember one thing about the world's end.'
segments.s169-g41: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بیدار شد is a past action: بیدار (awake) plus شد used as the past/simple perfect auxiliary 'awoke'. پهلوان سپاه is a noun phrase 'the champion of the army' (pahlavān = champion, سپاه = army/host) with the title following the head.
segments.s169-g42: دمان here is a proper name (the hero Daman) rather than the lexical senses given, so I mark the sense as none. اندر آمد is the simple past 'entered/came in' (اندر = in/into, آمد = came). به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'towards/near the king' specifying motion/direction.
segments.s169-g43: همی ماند is a durative past: همی adds progressive/continuative force ('kept, continued to') and ماند is the past 'remained/waited'. تا introduces the subordinate clause 'until', and جای پردخت شد is a passive-like or result clause 'the place was finished/prepared' (پردخت = finish/embellishment; شد = became/was).
segments.s169-g44: به نزدیک is a locative/directional phrase 'near'. آن نامور qualifies تخت: 'that famed/renowned (one) throne'. تخت شد is a stative/resultive use of شد after the noun, meaning 'a throne was set/placed' or 'the throne came to be (brought)'.
segments.s169-g45: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (be + pronoun) marking the indirect object/recipient. گفت here is the past 'said' (the lemma senses listed in the data are not the verbal past form, so I mark its listed senses as not matching). خورشیدفش مهترا are vocative epithets/names addressed directly.
segments.s169-g46: A string of vocative epithets joined by و: جهاندار 'world‑ruler/sovereign', بیدار used adjectivally as 'alert/awake', and افسونگرا an epithet 'one who uses/enacts enchantment' or 'sorcerous'. The conjunction و simply links the epithets.
segments.s169-g47: به در بر is a locative/directional phrase 'to/at the gate' (به = to/towards; در = gate; بر here functions directionally), یکی بنده is 'one/a certain servant' (یکی = a certain/one, بنده = servant). بفزود is a past 3rd‑person form 'he added/caused to be added' (ب‑ prefix + فزود), and دوش is the temporal adverb 'last night' modifying the action.
segments.s169-g48: تو گفتی is the 2nd‑person past 'you said' (the provided senses for گفتی do not list the simple-past meaning, so I mark that as not matching). ورا is the indirect object 'to him'. مایه دادست هوش is a small clause meaning 'gave him the mâyé (resource/substance) of hoosh (intelligence, awareness)' — دادست is an archaic past form 'gave' and هوش is the thing bestowed ('intelligence' or 'sense').
segments.s169-g9: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that/for' linking this clause to what precedes. روز نوآیین is a noun + adjective phrase 'a day of new custom' (نوآیین = new/novel) and جشنی نوست repeats the idea with an explicit copula: نوست is a poetic/archaic copula meaning 'is new' (نو + -ست, where -ست is the contracted form of است). The copula applies to the coordinated nouns, so the clause states that the day is new and the festival is new.
segments.s169-g10: شب is the subject 'the night'. سور آزاده کیخسروست is a nominal predicate: سور (feast) modified by the epithet آزاده ('noble‑hearted') and finished by کیخسروست, where -ست is the poetic form of است and the whole phrase means 'is (the) Kay Khosrow (’s)'. In other words, the clause says 'the night is the noble feast of Kay Khosrow' (آزاده modifies Kay Khosrow as an epithet attached before the predicate name).
segments.s169-g11: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed'. بلرزید is a past‑tense verb in Ferdowsi's poetic form ('trembled' — poetic preverb ب- plus past stem and -ید ending). در خواب خوش is an adverbial phrase 'in (a) pleasant sleep' (خوش modifies خواب). The word order puts subject + verb + adverbial, typical of Ferdowsi's line.
segments.s169-g12: بجنبید is another past action in the same poetic conjugation ('moved/stirred'). گلشهر is a proper name (the city) and خورشیدفش is an epithet attached to it meaning 'sun‑bright' or 'radiant'. Grammatically this is a simple past clause: 'Golshahr, radiant, moved/stirred.'
segments.s169-g13: بدو is the dative clitic phrase 'to him'. The line uses inverted poetic order: بدو گفت پیران literally 'to him said the elders' = 'the elders said to him'. که introduces the reported imperative that follows. برخیز and رو are imperatives: برخیز 'stand up / arise' and رو is the classical imperative 'go' (here not the noun 'face').
segments.s169-g14: خرامنده is a present‑participle/adverbial form 'striding/prancing', describing the manner of movement. پیش + فرنگیس means 'before/in the presence of Ferangis' (پیش = in front of / in the presence of). شو is the imperative of شدن 'become' used with a participle to mean 'assume that manner' — here 'be (or go) striding toward Ferangis.'
segments.s169-g15: سیاووش را uses the object marker را to make 'Siyâvash' a definite direct object. دیدم is 1st person past 'I saw'. اکنون 'now' places the time, and به خواب is the idiomatic expression 'in a dream' (به + خواب = in a dream/in sleep). The whole segment is 'I saw Siyâvash now in a dream.'
segments.s169-g16: درخشان‌تر is the comparative form 'more shining / brighter' formed with the -تر suffix. از introduces the standard of comparison (here 'than'). برسپهر آفتاب is a compact poetic phrase: بر = 'upon/on', سپهر = 'the firmament/sky', آفتاب = 'the sun' — together 'the sun upon the sky' (i.e. 'the sun across the heavens'), so the phrase means 'brighter than the sun on the firmament.'
segments.s169-g153: همی is a continuous/adverbial marker meaning 'continually' or 'still'; پرورانیدش is a past transitive form ('they raised/nurtured him') with the object pronoun -ش attached; اندر کنار is a locative phrase ('in/at the side', i.e. 'by his side') placed after the verb in poetic word order.
segments.s169-g154: بدو is a dative phrase 'to him' (bd + -o), marking who benefits; شادمان is an adjective/noun 'joyful' describing the following noun; گردش روزگار is a compound meaning 'the turning/wheel of fate or fortune' — گردش ('turn, circuit') + روزگار ('fate, times'), so the whole phrase means 'the merry turn of fortune' that falls to him.
segments.s169-g155: بدین ('with this' / 'thus') is an adverbial phrase introducing the result; نیز 'also' reinforces it; بگذشت is the past verb 'passed' (time passed); چندی modifies duration ('a short while'); سپهر is the subject following the verb in poetic order and here functions as a time/heavenal word indicating that some time elapsed.
segments.s169-g156: به مغز اندرون داشت is a clause meaning 'he kept in the core of his inner self' — به + مغز اندرون ('in the innermost core'); داشت is the past imperfective/past-tense verb 'had/kept (inwardly)'; با شاه مهر is the object phrase 'love/affection for the king' (با here 'toward/for', مهر = 'love/affection').
segments.s169-g157: شب تیره هنگام is a temporal phrase 'at the dark time of night' (شب + adjective تیره + هنگام 'at the time of'); آرام و خواب are nouns/conditions ('calm and sleep') describing the state of the place — the clause sets the nocturnal, quiet scene.
segments.s169-g158: کس is an indefinite subject 'someone'; آمد is the past verb 'came'; ز نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'from/nearby' indicating approach; the final word افراسیاب names that someone (apposition) — so 'someone came from nearby: Afrasiab.'
segments.s169-g159: بران is a discourse adverb 'thereupon/thereon'; تیرگی gives the locative 'in the darkness'; پهلوان را is the direct object with the object marker را ('the champion'); بخواند is a past tense verb here meaning 'he called/summoned' (one of its poetic senses) — so 'thereupon, in the darkness, he summoned the champion.'
segments.s169-g160: گذشته سخن‌ها is a noun phrase 'words/tales of the past' (گذشته = 'past', سخن‌ها = 'speeches/tales'); فراوان functions adverbially 'in great number' or 'plentifully'; براند is a past-form verb from راند 'to drive/bring forth' used here idiomatically to mean 'he put forth/uttered' — 'he set forth many past tales.'
segments.s169-g49: نماند is the past-form used impersonally meaning “there did not remain / nothing remained.” ز خوبی is an oblique genitive phrase “of goodness.” جز از تو is an exclusion phrase “except from you,” and به کس follows as a dative/indirect-object phrase “to anyone.” The word order is verb → oblique phrases (typical in classical/poetic Persian), yielding “Nothing of goodness remained to anyone except from you.”
segments.s169-g50: تو گویی is a present claim addressed to “you” (تو = “you” + گویی = “you say / you claim / you seem to say”); که introduces the subordinate clause. بر گاه is a locative phrase “upon the seat/throne (گاه used poetically),” شاهست is an archaic/poetic fused form meaning “is king / is at the king’s (seat),” and و بس is a final comment “and that is enough / and that is all.”
segments.s169-g51: اگر ... بازآمدی forms a conditional clause: اگر = “if,” بازآمدی is the past-form used hypothetically here (“you should return / you return”). تور را is written as a unit here; in context it functions as the object/topic (archaic spelling of تو را or a proper name), and را marks definiteness/emphasis. روز (“day”) places the temporal sense ‘one day’ — together the clause means “if on some day you return.”
segments.s169-g52: به دیدار چهرش نیاز آمدی is an existential/result clause where نیاز آمدن (“need came”) expresses ‘you will be in need.’ به دیدار = “to/for a meeting/visit,” چهرش = “his face” (چهر + enclitic ـش), and آمدی is the 2nd-person form used with نیاز آمدن to mean ‘you will come to be in need.’ The whole phrase: “you will find yourself in need to see his face / you will need to behold his face.”
segments.s169-g53: فریدون گردست is a nominal copular idea: فریدون (proper name) + گردست (bracelet/wrist ornament) = “Fereydun is a bracelet/ornament.” گویی بجای is a parenthetical “one might say / it seems, in place of” (گویی = ‘it is said / one would say’; بجای = “instead of / in the place of”), setting up the list that follows.
segments.s169-g54: This is a coordinated list introduced by repeated به (“to/for” or here roughly marking the items): به فر (the divine glory / royal splendour), و به چهر (and the face/appearance), و به دست (and the hand/power), و به پای (and the foot). The repetition of به and و is a poetic enumerative structure: these are the things for/which he stands in place.
segments.s169-g55: بر ایوان gives the locative “on the portico/veranda.” چنو (چنان) = “thus / in such a way” modifies the following negative/indefinite construction کس نبیند نگار: کس = “anyone/no one” and نبیند is a classical/poetic negative form of دیدن meaning “has not seen / does not see” (used here to mean no one had seen). نگار = “the beloved.” Together: “On the portico no one had seen the beloved thus.”
segments.s169-g56: بدو = “to him” (بِ + ضمیر enclitic), تازه شد = “became fresh / was renewed” (شد as the past of شدن), and فره شهریار = “the king’s farr / divine royal glory” (فره = farr, شهریار = sovereign/king). Word order places the verb before its logical subject (typical poetic flexibility): “To him the king’s farr became renewed.”
segments.s169-g33: ز بهر سیاوش is a prepositional phrase: ز بهر (literally ‘from/for the sake of’) gives the reason or purpose — here ‘because of/for Siyavash’. دو دیده پرآب is a noun phrase with numeral + noun + postposed descriptive compound (پرآب, literally ‘full of water’) used poetically for ‘tear-filled/teary eyes’.
segments.s169-g34: همی کرد نفرین بر افراسیاب is the main clause. همی before the verb gives the continuous/habitual epic flavour ('kept/was continually'); کرد is the light verb here combining with نفرین (نفرین کردن = 'to curse'). بر introduces the target of the curse (افراسیاب) with a sense close to 'against/on'.
segments.s169-g35: چنین گفت با نامدار انجمن: چنين is an adverb 'thus/so'; گفت functions as the speech-verb ('said') and با + انجمن ('with/at the assembly') gives the audience. نامدار qualifies انجمن ('the renowned assembly').
segments.s169-g36: که‌گر (a contracted poetic form of که اگر, 'if') introduces a conditional clause. بگسلد is formed with the prefix ب- plus the verb گسَلد (here in the optative/subjunctive sense) meaning 'should break/tear apart'. زین سخن ('this speech', zīn = archaic/poetic 'this/of this') is the object phrase; جان من ('my life') is the thing threatened by that action. The clause thus reads 'if by this speech my life were to be broken'.
segments.s169-g37: نمانم is a first‑person negative form meaning 'I do not remain / I would not remain' (classical/poetic negative). که begins the subordinate conditional/explanatory clause. یازد is a present/subjunctive form of یازیدن here with the sense 'to strike/lay hold' (poetic). بدین شاه چنگ is a compact phrase 'with this/so — the king's claw/grip' (بدین an archaic/poetic 'with/this' + شاه چنگ 'the king's claw/grasp').
segments.s169-g38: مرا‌گر is a conditional locution ('if he were to give me / should he hand me over'); سپارد is the verb سپردن in the appropriate tense/mood meaning 'to entrust/hand over'; به چنگ نهنگ is a prepositional phrase 'into the claws/grasp of the whale' (a hyperbolic image). The clause restates the feared outcome: being delivered to the whale's clutches.
segments.s169-g39: بدانگه is a temporal adverb 'at that time/thereupon'; که introduces the temporal clause 'when'. بنمود is the past verb 'showed/revealed' (بنمودن), and خورشید چهر is an inverted noun phrase 'the sun (its) face' — 'the sun showed its face.'
segments.s169-g40: به خواب اندرآمد is a verb phrase meaning 'entered into (the) dream' (به خواب = into dream/during sleep; اندرآمد = entered/appeared). سر تیره مهر is a nominal phrase with modifiers after the head in poetic ordering: سر ('head/visage') modified by تیره ('dark/ominous') and مهر (here a personal/ divine name or personified entity, 'Mehr'), yielding 'the dark-visaged Mehr' who appears in the dream.
segments.s169-g129: نشست is a simple past verb 'he sat down' (نشست = sat). The following short prepositional string از بر باره دست‌کش functions as an adverbial phrase: از بر literally 'from off/upon' (poetic/older usage), باره is an archaic noun here forming part of the phrase, and دست‌کش 'glove' is the noun being referred to — literally 'the glove from off (his) person/side.'
segments.s169-g130: بیامد is past 'he came' (came forward). بر here means 'toward / to (the presence of)', so بیامد بر [X] = 'came before / approached X.' خسرو is the proper name Khosrow, and شیرفش is an epithet/name attached to him (treated here as a name rather than a literal noun).
segments.s169-g131: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / that,' and پیش او شد is a light-verb construction where پیش = 'in the presence of / before' and شد functions as the auxiliary 'became / was made' → 'was brought before him / came into his presence.' به مهر is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'with affection.'
segments.s169-g132: نگه کرد is a verbal phrase 'cast a gaze / looked' where نگه (gaze/look) combines with کرد (to make/do) to mean 'he looked.' پیران is the doer here ('Piran' or 'the elders' depending on context) placed before the verb (typical SOV/OV flexibility in poetry). بران 'on/at that' links to فر و چهر 'that glory and face' as the objects of the gaze.
segments.s169-g133: به بر درگرفتش is a compact clause where درگرفتن (here in past form + clitic) means 'to seize / to take hold of / to embrace'; the prefixed به بر emphasizes 'into/onto his bosom' or 'in his arms.' زمانی دراز 'for a long time' is an adverbial phrase (زمانی = 'for a while', دراز = 'long').
segments.s169-g134: همی گفت is an iterative/frame-marked form meaning 'he kept saying / he was saying.' با introduces the partner in speech 'with,' and داور پاک راز is the phrase 'the pure judge' (داور پاک) followed by راز 'secret' — together indicating a confidential conversation (i.e. 'he spoke confidentially with the pure judge').
segments.s169-g135: بدو گفت is an inversion placing the indirect object first: بدو 'to him' + گفت 'said' (here simple past). کیخسرو پاک‌دین is the speaking subject with an epithet: کیخسرو (Kai Khosrow) and پاک‌دین 'pure‑of‑faith / devout', forming the nominative phrase 'Kai Khosrow the pious.'
segments.s169-g136: به تو باد is a standard optative formula: به تو 'to you' + باد (optative particle 'may') expressing a wish. رخشنده modifies 'توران زمین' and means 'radiant / shining'; توران زمین = 'the land of Turan.' Together: 'May the land of Turan be radiant for you.'
segments.s169-g73: کنون 'now' opens the line. بودنی is a deverbal/nominal form meaning 'that which is (to be)' or 'being'; هرچ 'whatever' modifies بودنی. بایست is a modal 'must/ought' and is followed by بود 'to be' (here the copula/infinitive sense), so the clause literally reads 'now whatever is-to-be must be' = 'whatever will happen will happen.' Word order is topicalized (time + nominal + modifier + modal + copula).
segments.s169-g74: ندارد is the negative present 'does not have' (or 'there is no'). The following coordinated nouns غم و رنج و اندیشه list the sources (sorrow, suffering, thought), and سود 'benefit' appears at the end as the item that is lacking. The literal order 'ندارد ... سود' = 'there is no benefit in sorrow, suffering, or thought.'
segments.s169-g75: مداریدش is the verb مدارَ‌اَزد (مدارا کردن) with a plural/imperative/optative ending and the enclitic -ش 'him'—'show forbearance toward him' or 'treat him kindly.' اندر میان گروه is a prepositional phrase 'in the midst of the group/company' (اندر + میان + گروه). The verb takes the object suffix -ش and the prepositional phrase gives location.
segments.s169-g76: به نزد شبانان is a destination/benefactive phrase 'to/into the presence of the shepherds' (به + نزد + شبانان). فرستش is فرست + enclitic -ش 'send him' (imperative/command). The final به کوه 'to the mountain' gives the place; classical Persian can stack such prepositional phrases before and after the verb.
segments.s169-g77: بدان here is built from the prefix ب- + دان (see word split) and functions as an instruction 'give/hand over' or 'to that end' depending on context; it introduces a purpose clause. تا introduces purpose 'so that' and is followed by نداند, the negative third-person form 'he/they do not know' (subjunctive/optative sense of purpose). که من خود کیم is an indirect question/relative clause 'that I myself am who' = 'who I am' (کیم is an archaic/poetic interrogative 'who').
segments.s169-g78: بدیشان is 'to them' (به + ایشان contracted) marking the indirect object. سپرده is the past participle/perfect of سپردن 'entrusted/placed'—'I have entrusted (him) to them' (subject 'I' is implied). ز بهر چیم is a short interrogative/reflective phrase: ز بهر 'for the sake of / on account of' and چیم ('چهٔ من' contracted) meaning 'what of me' or 'what concern/purpose of mine'—a rhetorical question about motive.
segments.s169-g79: نیاموزد is the negative present/jussive form of آموختن and here functions as an instruction or result clause 'let not ... learn / should not be taught.' از کس 'from anyone' or 'by anyone' indicates the agent/source; خرد‌گر 'the wise one' (خرد + گر, an agentive) is the person who should not learn; نژاد 'lineage' is the object. The clause means 'let no wise person learn his lineage.'
segments.s169-g80: ز کار گذشته is a prepositional phrase 'from/of deeds past' (ز + کار + گذشته). نیایدش is the negative present/subjunctive 'does not come' with enclitic -ش 'to him' (i.e. 'no remembrance comes to him'). یاد 'remembrance/memory' appears as the noun that does not come. The phrase states that past acts will yield him no remembrance (so do not let learned people know his origin).
segments.s169-g137: This clause begins with the name-like word 'ازیرا' (treated here as a topicalized proper name). 'کسی' is an indefinite subject ('no one' / 'anyone'), 'کت' is the direct object (here the rare noun 'kit' understood as a book/item), and 'نداند' is the third-person negative present (classical Persian) meaning 'does not/does know'. The enclitic 'همی' is a classical/poetic marker indicating an ongoing or general/habitual sense ('ever/indeed'); word order (Name — indefinite subject — object — verb — همی) is typical of older Persian poetry where topicalized elements precede the verbal clause.
segments.s169-g138: 'جز از' introduces an exception ('except from/by'), 'مهربانت' is 'your kindness' with the possessive suffix -ت, and 'نخواند' is the third-person negative present of خواندن here used in the sense 'to read/recite' (the verb is used impersonally/passively in translation: 'will not be read'). The trailing 'همی' again gives the habitual/ongoing poetic sense. Grammatically this clause limits the previous statement: whatever the 'کت' is, it will not be read except by means of (or through) your kindness.
segments.s169-g139: 'شبان‌زاده‌ای' is an indefinite noun phrase 'a shepherd's son'; the object/topic marker 'را' is used here to topicalize or mark the person who will be placed. 'چنین در کنار' ('thus at [your] side') specifies how/where to place him. In older poetic Persian the object/topic 'X را' can precede a verb that follows later; 'چنین' modifies the following action and 'در کنار' functions as a locative.
segments.s169-g140: 'بگیری' is a second-person singular present/subjunctive (classical form) meaning 'you take/you should take' — here used as counsel or instruction. The conjunction 'و' links the consequent clause. 'از کس' = 'from anyone', with 'کس' indefinite, and 'نیایدت' is the negative present ('does not come') with the enclitic '-ت' marking 'to you' (dative). 'عار' is the subject/complement 'shame/disgrace'; the whole clause means 'and shame will not come to you from anyone' i.e. 'you will have no shame before anyone.'
segments.s169-g141: The phrase 'خردمند را دل' places the possessor/topic ('the wise man') before the possessed noun 'دل' ('heart') with the object/topic marker 'را' — a common poetic ordering meaning 'the wise man's heart'. 'برو' here functions as an adverbial/archaic particle tied to the action (in older Persian contexts it can mean roughly 'for him' / 'thereupon'), 'بر' is a local/contracted preposition 'upon/at', and 'بسوخت' is a past tense verb 'burned' (perfective). The clause thus reads 'he burned the wise man's heart' with the topicalized possessor up front.
segments.s169-g142: 'به کردار' = 'in the manner/like', where 'کردار' is being used as 'manner' or 'mode'. 'آتش رخش' is a genitive phrase 'the fire of Rakhsh' (Rakhsh being the famous steed), and 'برفروخت' is a past tense verb formed with the prefix بر- plus فروخت meaning here 'kindled/ignited' (poetic sense). Together the clause is a simile: 'he kindled it like Rakhsh's fire.'
segments.s169-g143: 'بدو گفت' is a short word-order inversion of 'به او گفت' — 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it'). 'کای' is the classical vocative 'O', and 'یادگار مهان' is a vocative noun phrase 'remembrance/memento of the chiefs (mehan)'. Grammatically the clause opens the quoted address: 'He said to him, "O ..."'.
segments.s169-g144: 'پسندیده' is an adjective 'pleasing/beloved' describing the addressee; the coordinating 'و' links the next adjective. 'ناسپرده' is an older/poetic adjective meaning 'unentrusted' or 'unrevealed/untold' (here 'not given or shared'), and 'جهان' is the noun 'world' (the domain in which this quality is asserted). Together they form the second half of the vocative: 'beloved and untold in the world.'
