segments.s169-g193: This clause is a compact past-action clause: پیران (here an honorific singular 'the elder') is the subject, ببوسید is an archaic past form 'kissed' (3rd‑person), and را is the definite object marker attached to زمین ('the earth/the ground'). Word order is Subject Verb Object (subject may appear after the verb in older Persian poetry), so literally 'the elder kissed the ground.' گفت immediately follows to introduce direct speech.
segments.s169-g194: که‌ای is an archaic vocative combining که + ای meaning 'O' (address marker). دادگر is an epithet 'just' used adjectivally before شاه 'king/ruler'. بی‌یار means 'without friends/companion' and جفت 'mate/spouse' completes the description; the conjunction و links the two descriptors. The whole phrase is a vocative apposition: 'O just king, friendless and without a mate.'
segments.s169-g195: برین is the fused older form of بر این 'by/on this' (بر + این); بند here means 'bond' (a pledge/obligation) and سوگند 'oath', so the phrase 'برین بند و سوگند' functions as an ablative/instrumental ('by this bond and oath'). تو ایمنم places the addressee/topic 'you' before the predicate; ایمنم is present 'I am safe/secure (with you).' The clause therefore reads 'By this bond and oath, I am safe with you.'
segments.s169-g196: کنون is an adverb 'now/presently' setting time. یافت is the past 'found/obtained' (here used intransitively or as a perfect of experience). آرام qualifies what was found: 'rest/calm,' and جان و تنم 'my soul and my body' are the entities that have obtained that state. The clause thus means 'now my soul and body have found rest.' Note: the lemma list lacked a suitable sense for تنم (my body), so that sense is not in the provided options.
segments.s169-g197: وزانجا is a compound adverb 'and/from there.' بر here functions as 'toward/to' with آمد 'came' (arrived toward Khosrow). آمد is simple past 'came.' دمان (دمان) is an object/noun 'skirt/hem' (the apparel that arrived/approached), so the literal sense is 'and from there the skirt/hem came to Khosrow' — a poetic way to say she approached him, focusing on her trailing skirt.
segments.s169-g198: رخی is derived from رخ 'face' used adjectivally/adverbially ('with a face ...') and is followed by the color ارغوان 'crimson/purple' — noun + modifier ordering typical in Persian poetic phrasing ('a face crimson'). Likewise دلی شادمان is 'a heart joyful' (دلی 'heart', شادمان 'happy'). Together these coordinate phrases describe her appearance and mood: 'her face crimson and her heart joyful.'
segments.s169-g199: بدو گفت is a two‑word reporting phrase: بدو 'to him/unto him' (dative) plus گفت 'said' (past). The embedded clause begins with کز here meaning 'from/that from' (ک + ز), introducing the content: دل خرد 'wisdom of the heart' and دور کن is an imperative formed from دور 'away' + کن 'do' — 'make (it) distant/remove (it).' So the command is 'Remove wisdom from (your) heart' or more idiomatically 'drive prudence from your heart.'
segments.s169-g200: چو is the subordinating 'when/if.' رزم آورد is 'brings battle' (آورد past 'brought/caused to come'). پاسخش 'his/its answer' is the object ( پاسخش = پاسخ + -ش ), and سور کن is an imperative 'make (it) a feast' (سور = feast/banquet, کن = do!). The clause advises: 'When battle comes, make its answer/response a feast' — a proverbial, figurative instruction to meet violence with celebration or to treat the outcome as a revel.
segments.s169-g257: در گنج‌های کهن کرد باز is a compact, poetic word order: در + noun phrase gives the location ('in/inside the ancient treasuries'); کرد + باز is an inverted/poetic ordering of باز کرد (i.e. 'opened' or 'made open'). Here کرد is the past tense verb 'did/made' and باز functions as the verbal complement 'opened' (poetic inversion).
segments.s169-g258: ز هر گونه‌ای marks the source/material ('from every kind/type'); شاه را is the definite object with the postposed object-marker را (marks 'the king' as the object); کرد ساز is a light/compound verbal construction where کرد (to do/make) combines with ساز (noun) to mean 'made/prepared/equipped'—poetic phrasing for 'furnished the king'.
segments.s169-g259: This is an enumerative list introduced by the poetic preposition ز ('from'). The repeated و simply joins items (dinar, diybā/brocade, sword, jewel); there is no main verb inside this segment — it continues the inventory of things supplied.
segments.s169-g260: A parallel continuation of the list, again prefaced by ز 'from' and joining items with و. Each noun names another category of equipment (horse, arms, helmet, belt); the segment remains a series of nouns forming part of the things provided.
segments.s169-g261: هم از is the simple additive 'also from'; تخت is 'throne'. وز here is a poetic connective standing for 'and' (an archaic/poetic form rather than the common و) linking تخت to بدره‌های درم; بدره‌های درم is a genitive/compound (badré-hā-ye dirm) meaning silver/dirham ornaments or round silver pieces — a poetic name for precious silver items.
segments.s169-g262: ز گستردنی‌ها 'from the spreadables/coverings' (an abstract plural noun meaning items one spreads out, e.g. carpets/cloths), و از بیش و کم is an idiomatic pair meaning 'and from more and less' (i.e. from things large and small / from everything). The prepositions and conjunctions organize a final, catch‑all part of the inventory.
segments.s169-g263: گسی is an indefinite pronoun/adverb meaning 'some' or 'a certain (party)'. کردشان is a contracted/poetic form meaning 'caused them' or 'made them (go)'—here the verb + enclitic indicates action upon 'them' (the things/people mentioned earlier); سوی آن شارستان means 'toward that city' (سوی = toward, آن = that, شارستان = city).
segments.s169-g264: کجا here functions as a relative 'where/which' introducing a descriptive clause about the city; جملگی 'altogether' + گشته (perfect/participial use of گشتن) 'had become' gives the result state; بد خارستان = 'a bad/ill (بد) thorn‑thicket (خارستان)' — i.e. the city had wholly turned into a hostile, thorny waste. This is a compact relative clause describing the city's condition.
segments.s169-g17: Here كي/که begins an interrogative/relative clause ('who/that'); گفتی is a past-form utterance ('you/he said') introducing reported speech; مرا is the object pronoun (م + را, with را marking the definite object); چند modifies the following epithet خسپی as an interrogative 'how/what (kind of)'; the whole clause is a reproachful question. مپای is an abrupt exclamation/imperative (archaic) interrupting the speech, roughly 'do not!' or 'no, stop!'.
segments.s169-g18: به جشن is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the feast'; جهانجوی is a compound adjective 'world-seeking' qualifying the following proper name کیخسرو; آی is an imperative/vocative 'come!' (an archaic short imperative used to invite). The word order is typical Persian: prepositional phrase + modifier + name + imperative.
segments.s169-g19: همی before the verb marks an ongoing/habitual past ('kept on'); رفت is the simple past 'went'; گلشهر is the place-name object; تا introduces the extent ('as far as/until'); پیش ماه literally 'before/in the presence of the moon' (ماه here used poetically of a beloved/beautiful one).
segments.s169-g20: جدا گشته بود is a periphrastic past (perfect) meaning 'had become separated' (گشته = perfect participle, بود as auxiliary); از بر ماه is an older locative/origin phrase 'from the side/presence of the moon' where از marks separation and بر 'on/upon' is part of the idiom; شاه at the end is a vocative address 'O king'.
segments.s169-g21: بدید is a past tense 'he saw'; و links clauses; به شادی is a prepositional phrase 'in/with joy'; سبک here is an adjectival/adverbial 'light (in spirit), carefree'; بازگشت is the past verb 'returned.' Word order places manner (به شادی سبک) before the verb بازگشت.
segments.s169-g22: همانگاه is an adverb 'immediately'; گیتی 'the world' is the subject; پرآواز गشت is a predicate meaning 'became full-voiced / renowned' (گشت = became). The compound means the world at once rang out or became celebrated.
segments.s169-g23: بیامد is past 'he came'; به شادی indicates the manner 'in joy'; به پیران marks the indirect object 'to the elders'; بگفت is the past-reporting verb 'he said' introducing direct speech. The sequence is typical: verb (came) + manner phrase + indirect object + reporting verb.
segments.s169-g24: که introduces the reported clause ('that'); اینت is an archaic/dialectal demonstrative 'this one'; به‌آیین is an adverbial phrase 'according to rite / properly'; خور و ماه جفت states the predicate 'the sun and moon (are) a pair/match' (جفت = mate/pair). The clause is a declarative identification (this is the match of sun and moon).
segments.s169-g273: This clause expresses a wish using the optative particle 'باد' at the end. 'ز' means 'from/away from' and introduces the source from which something should be distant. 'شاه کیان' is a compound referring to the Kayan kings (possessive/genitival phrase). 'چشم بد' is an idiom 'evil eye' (literally 'bad eye'). Word order is emphatic/poetic: 'From the Kayan kings (ز شاه کیان) [let] the evil eye (چشمِ بد) be far (دور) — باد marks the optative 'may it be'.
segments.s169-g274: 'روان' here means 'soul/spirit' and is the subject; 'سیاوش' names whose soul it is. 'پر از' is the fixed combination 'full of'; 'نور' is the content ('light'). The line ends with the optative 'باد' = 'may it be', so the whole segment reads 'May the soul of Siavash be full of light.' Word order places the predicate 'پر از نور' before the optative verb 'باد' in typical poetic fashion.
segments.s169-g275: Literal order: 'همه خاکِ آن شارستان شاد شد' = 'All the earth of that city became joyful.' 'همه خاک' is the subject ('all the soil/earth'), 'آن شارستان' qualifies which earth ('that city''s earth'), and 'شاد شد' is the past verbal phrase 'became glad' where 'شد' is the past of 'become'.
segments.s169-g276: Here the phrase is compressed/poetic: 'گیا بر چمن سرو آزاد شد' — 'on the meadow the vegetation / a plant, the cypress, became free.' 'گیا' (plant/vegetation) and the locative 'بر چمن' set the scene, and 'سرو آزاد شد' is the focal clause 'the cypress became free', with 'شد' as past 'became'. The inversion emphasizes the result (the liberated cypress).
segments.s169-g277: 'ز' = 'from' introduces the source. 'خاکی' denotes the earth/soil (here used as noun) and is modified by the relative clause introduced by 'که'. In that clause 'خون سیاوش بخورد' uses 'بخورد' (poetic/past perfective) meaning 'drank/absorbed' (the soil took in the blood). So the phrase means 'from the soil which absorbed Siavash's blood'.
segments.s169-g278: Order: 'به ابر اندرآمد درختی ز گرد' = 'into the cloud entered a tree from the dust.' 'به ابر' is locative 'into/up to the clouds', 'اندرآمد' is an archaic perfective verb meaning 'entered/rose up', and 'درختی ز گرد' is an indefinite noun phrase 'a tree from dust' (ز = 'from', گرد = 'dust'). The line thus depicts a tree rising out of the dust into the clouds.
segments.s169-g279: 'نگاریده' is a past-participle/adjectival form meaning 'painted/depicted' and functions here as a predicate modifier. 'بر برگ‌ها' is the locative phrase 'on the leaves'. 'چهر او' ('his face') is the thing depicted. Word order places the participle before the location and the possessed noun, yielding 'His face was depicted on the leaves.'
segments.s169-g280: 'همه' here intensifies ('all/entirely'); 'بوی مشک' is the subject 'the scent of musk'; 'آمد' is the past 'came' (resultative). 'از مهر او' is an ablative/source phrase 'from his love' (indicating cause or origin). The clause means 'the whole scent of musk came from his love/affection.'
segments.s169-g105: چو is the archaic/poetic conjunction 'when'. شد is the simple past of شدن 'to become'. هفت ساله is a predicative age phrase 'seven years old' placed after the verb (common in Persian). گو here appears as an epithet or name-like element attached to سرفراز (poetic usage); in this line it functions as a label/epithet before سرفراز, so the whole phrase reads 'when (he) became seven years old, the (one) called/epitheted سرفراز (the proud)'.
segments.s169-g106: هنر is used as a noun (personified here) and is the agent of the clause. با + نژادش is a prepositional phrase 'with / to his neshād (lineage, kin)' where -ش is the 3rd-person possessive 'his'. همی is the archaic progressive/frequentative particle (here 'would / used to') placed before the verb. گفت is used in its verbal sense 'spoke / told' and راز is the direct object 'secret(s)'. The word order is typical poetical Persian: agent (هنر) + oblique phrase (با نژادش) + verbal phrase (همی گفت راز).
segments.s169-g107: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. چوبی modifies کمان: 'a bow of wood' (چوبی = wooden/of wood). کرد is the past tense 'made' (کردن used as a light verb). وز is here a poetic contracted sequence (و + ز) meaning 'and from', i.e. 'and from'. روده 'gut/intestinal casing' names the material used and زه is 'the bowstring'; the verb is understood to apply to both objects ('made a bow of wood and (made) the string from gut').
segments.s169-g108: ز (again 'from') هر سو 'every direction / on every side' is an adverbial phrase. برافگند is a past-tense verbal form (literal root 'افکندن' = 'to throw'); in this context poetic usage means 'fastened / threw (the string) taut / arranged' — here translated 'fastened'. زه را uses the object-marker را to mark 'the string' as definite object. گره 'knot' is the noun receiving the action: 'he tied knots on the string' (word order in poetry places the verb before its object and modifiers).
segments.s169-g109: ابی is an adjective 'blue' modifying the following nouns. پر و پیکان is a coordinated noun phrase 'feather and arrow' (in practice describing a fletched arrow). یکی تير places the numeral before تیر: 'one arrow' or 'a single shaft'. کرد is the past tense 'made'/'prepared'. In poetic description adjectives may modify a compound (ابی modifies both پر and پیکان).
segments.s169-g110: به دشت اندر is an adverbial locution 'into the plain' (به + دشت with the poetic اندر 'in/into'). آهنگ here is the noun sense 'aim/intention' (not 'melody'), so اندر آهنگ نخچیر کرد means 'he went with the intention/aim of (taking) نخچیر (game/prey)'; نخچیر is the object 'game' and کرد is the light verb 'did / performed' that combines with نخچیر to mean 'hunted'. Word order is typical: locative phrase first, then purpose/aim phrase plus verb.
segments.s169-g111: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. ده‌ساله شد is the usual pattern 'became ten years old'. گشت is a second verbal predicate 'became/turned into' (often used to intensify the previous predicate). گردی here carries the sense of roaming/wandering or a roaming habit and سترگ (literally 'big') qualifies it as 'great' or 'wide-ranging' — together forming a descriptive predicate 'he became a great rover / his roaming (or gaze) became great'. Poetic Persian often stacks predicates like this for emphasis.
segments.s169-g112: به is the directional/locative preposition 'to/at'. زخم here is 'wound' but in hunting context the construction به زخمِ گراز can mean 'coming upon the boar (so as to wound it)' or more loosely 'came upon boar'; گراز 'boar' is the animal encountered. آمد is the simple past 'came/arrived'. The sequence 'و خرس و گرگ' adds 'and bear and wolf' — three animals encountered. In short: he came upon/encountered boar, bear, and wolf (the verbs and objects are arranged poetically, with آمد governing the sequence).
segments.s169-g297: بجای عنانم عصا داد سال is a compact clause where بجای (preposition) heads the phrase 'in place of / instead of', عنانم ('my reins') is the direct object of the contrast, عصا داد is a past action ('gave a staff'), and سال (here personified) is the agent: 'Age gave...'. Classical Persian often places the agent (سال) after the verb for metrical reasons.
segments.s169-g298: پراگنده شد مال is a short result clause: پراگنده (adjective) + شد (past of شدن 'became/was') gives 'his property became scattered'. و links to برگشت حال where برگشت (a noun/verb form) + حال ('state/condition') describes a reversal or change of state — 'and his condition turned/changed'. The verbs are simple past/perfective in sense.
segments.s169-g299: همان (demonstrative 'that/the same') modifies دیده‌بان ('watchman/sentinel'); بر سر کوهسار is a prepositional locative ('on the summit of the mountain'). Word order is typical of poetry: modifier + noun, then the locative phrase.
segments.s169-g300: نبیند همی لشکر شهریار uses نبیند (negative present of دیدن, 'does not see') with the adverb همی giving continuous/emphatic aspect ('keeps/indeed does not see'). لشکر شهریار is the object phrase 'the king's army/host'. Note the poetic word order (verb before adverb and object).
segments.s169-g301: کشیدن ز دشمن نداند عنان has the infinitive/gerund کشیدن ('to pull') governed by نداند ('does not know'). ز (poetic 'from') + دشمن ('the enemy') forms the prepositional complement 'how to pull away from the enemy'. عنان ('the reins') is the grammatical subject placed after the verb for emphasis — 'the reins do not know (how to be pulled)'.
segments.s169-g302: مگر پیش مژگانش آید سنان is a conditional/exemptive clause introduced by مگر ('unless'). پیش مژگانش ('before his eyelashes') is a spatial idiom meaning 'before his eyes/face'; آید ('comes') is the verb and سنان ('spear') is the subject — 'unless a spear comes before his eyelashes'.
segments.s169-g303: گراینده تیزپای نوند is a nominal phrase: گراینده ('one who directs/guide') modified by the adjective تیزپای ('swift‑footed') with نوند likely a proper name or rare term attached as the head. In classical poetry proper names or epithets often appear without linking particles.
segments.s169-g304: همان شست بدخواه کردش به بند reads with همان ('that same') pointing to شست (here literally 'thumb' but possibly a nickname — the line is ambiguous). بدخواه ('ill‑wisher') is an appositive/agent and کردش is the past verb 'did/put' with the clitic -ش = 'him' as object; به بند ('into fetters') is the result phrase. Grammatically: subject (همان شست/ill‑wisher) + verb (کردش) + goal (به بند).
segments.s169-g97: نباید is an impersonal modal in the past-tense form used here as 'ought not / should not be'. که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. تنگ آیدش روزگار is a small clause with تنگ ('narrow, constrained') as predicate and آیدش ('comes to him' — آید + enclitic -ش) as verb; روزگار ('fate, fortune') is the theme/subject of the predicate, so literally 'it should not be that fate comes narrow upon him.'
segments.s169-g98: اگر is the conditional 'if'. دیده و دل form a coordinated phrase 'eye and heart' (two faculties). کند is the third-person past of کردن used here causatively/compatibly as 'make' or 'become' governing the predicate خواستار, so the structure is 'if eye and heart make (him) desirous' (i.e. 'if his eye and heart become desirous'). خواستار is an adjectival predicate 'desirous/longing'.
segments.s169-g99: شبان را uses را to mark 'the shepherd' as the definite recipient/topic; ببخشید is a past-tense transitive verb here in the sense 'bestowed/gave' (an older literary use), and بسیار چیز is the direct object phrase 'many things'. Word order places the indirect object/topic before the verb for emphasis: 'to the shepherd he gave many things.'
segments.s169-g100: یکی is an indefinite determiner 'a certain / one'; دایه is the noun 'nurse'. با او ('with him') is an instrumental/ accompanimental prepositional phrase modifying فرستاد, which is the simple past 'sent'. نیز at the end is an adverb 'also' linking this action to the previous one.
segments.s169-g101: بریشان is the fused sequence بر + ایشان ('to/onto them') placed before the verb for topicality; سپرد is the past-tense verb 'entrusted/handed over'. آن دل و دیده را is the definite object 'that heart and eye', with را marking the object. The overall order is indirect object/topic (بریشان) + verb (سپرد) + object (آن دل و دیده را).
segments.s169-g102: This is an appositive/identifying phrase for the recipient: جهانجوی is a compound noun 'world-seeker / ambitious person'. گرد پسندیده functions as a poetic epithet modifying that noun (گرد here participates in an adjectival phrase with پسندیده, giving a sense of being much-liked/esteemed); را again is the object marker tying this phrase to the verb 'سپرد' — so 'to the chosen/esteemed world-seeker.'
segments.s169-g103: بدین is the adverbial 'by/with this' (here written as ب + دین in the segmentation) introducing the manner; نیز is 'also'. بگذشت is the past verb 'passed (by/away)', and گردان سپهر is a noun phrase 'the revolving/turning heavens' with گردان as an attributive adjective modifying سپهر. Together: 'by this also the revolving heavens passed.'
segments.s169-g104: به خسرو is a dative phrase 'to Khosrow'; بر is an old directional/prepositional element 'upon/toward' strengthening the relation to the recipient. از مهر is an ablative or causal phrase 'out of love / from affection' modifying the verb. بخشود is the past tense verb 'granted/bestowed' and چهر is the object 'countenance/face' (here meaning a gracious or favorable appearance). Word order places the beneficiary phrases before the verb and the object immediately after it: 'to Khosrow, out of affection, (it) bestowed a face.'
segments.s169-g321: منم is the contracted present form ‘I am’ (من + هستم) used as the copula. بنده is a humble self-reference (‘servant’), and اهل‌بیت نبی is a genitive phrase ‘the household of the Prophet’ (noun + modifier). Word order is subject–copula–predicate: ‘I am (منم) [a] servant of the Prophet’s household.’
segments.s169-g322: This is a nominal apposition/epithet: سراینده ‘the poet’ names the speaker. خاک پای وصی is a chained genitive: خاک (dust) of پای (the foot) of وصی (the successor) — an idiom meaning humble devotion (‘the dust beneath the successor’s feet’). The noun chain is head-first then possessors follow: خاکِ پایِ وصی.
segments.s169-g323: برین is an archaic/poetic locative (بر + این) ‘on/at this (place)’. زادم is a first‑person past/inchoative form meaning ‘I was born’ (classical phrasing). و هم links the second clause (‘and also’) and بگذرم (classical present/subjunctive form) means ‘I shall pass / I will pass away’. The clause thus sequences location + past birth and a corresponding future/passive idea.
segments.s169-g324: چنان دان is an imperative/optative turn: چنان ‘thus’ + دان (imperative of ‘consider/hold’) = ‘consider it so’. که introduces the subordinate content clause. خاک پی حیدرم is a verbless clause where خاک (dust) is predicative and پی حیدر ‘at the foot/of Haydar’ is a locative/genitive modifier; the final -م on حیدر is the speaker’s copular/enclitic ending linking the clause to ‘I’ (elliptically: ‘(I am) the dust at Haydar’s feet’).
segments.s169-g325: ابا is an archaic verb/noun meaning ‘to refuse / be unwilling’. The following دیگران names the people concerned (‘others’). مر and مرا are first‑person pronouns in oblique/accusative positions used here for emphasis/dative flavour (‘for me / as for me’), and کار نیست is the negated predicate ‘there is no affair / I have no business’. Overall: ‘I refuse others; for me there is no business with them.’
segments.s169-g326: بدین اندرون is a locative phrase: بدین (classical contraction of به + این) ‘in/at this’ + اندرون ‘inner place/inner circle’. هیچ گفتار نیست is a standard existential negative: ‘there is no speech/talking at all’ (هیچ = none/emphatic negation, گفتار = speech, نیست = does not exist). The verb stands after the noun phrase as is common in Persian.
segments.s169-g327: به گفتار دهقان is an adverbial phrase ‘by/according to the saying of the dehqan’ (به + noun). کنون is an adverb ‘now’ and بازگرد is an imperative form ‘return’ (command). Word order places the adverbial phrase before the imperative for emphasis: ‘By the dehqan’s word, now return.’
segments.s169-g328: نگر is an imperative ‘look!’ or ‘see’. تا here functions like ‘so that / to’ introducing the content of what to look for: تا چه گوید = ‘to see what he says’. گوید is a third‑person present/subjunctive ‘he says’ (classical usage), and سراینده مرد is the subject phrase ‘the (man‑)poet’ placed after the verb for poetic word order: ‘see what the man‑poet says.’
segments.s169-g241: بدو گفت کاین دل ندارد بجای = 'He said to him that this heart has no steadiness.' بدو is a dative/enclitic phrase meaning 'to him'. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here introducing reported speech) and کاین introduces the subordinate clause 'that this...'. دل is the noun 'heart' (inner feeling), ندارد is the present negative 'does not have', and بجای ('in/place/stead') describes the lacking quality: lack of steadiness or fixed place.
segments.s169-g242: ز سر پرسمش پاسخ آرد ز پای = 'If I ask him about the head, he brings an answer from the foot.' ز سر is a prepositional phrase 'from/about the head' (head = subject/mind); پرسمش is the fused verb+object 'I ask him' (classical form with enclitic -ش). پاسخ آرد is a present/simple clause 'he brings/returns an answer', and ز پای ('from the foot') contrasts with 'ز سر', expressing that his answers come from a different place (i.e. are inconsistent or evasive).
segments.s169-g243: نیاید همانا بد و نیک ازوی = 'Truly (همانا), neither bad nor good comes from him.' نیاید is a negative present/impersonal 'does not come' (ن + آید); همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/indeed'; بد و نیک lists the two opposites 'bad and good'; ازوی ('ازِ وی' contracted) means 'from him'.
segments.s169-g244: نه زین‌سان بود مردم کینه‌جوی = 'The people were not of this sort, revenge-seeking.' نه negates the following predicate ('were not so'); زین‌سان is a demonstrative/adverbial 'thus/so'; بود is the past copula 'were/was'; مردم is the subject 'the people' (here postposed for emphasis), and کینه‌جوی is a predicative adjective 'vengeful, revenge-seeking'.
segments.s169-g245: رو این را به خوبی به مادر سپار = 'Go/turn, entrust this with kindness to the mother.' رو is an imperative verb 'go/turn' used as a directive; این را is the definite object 'this' (را marks it); به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'with kindness' or 'well'; به مادر سپار is the verbal command 'entrust to the mother' with سپار as the imperative 'entrust/hand over'. Word order places the object before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s169-g246: به دست یکی مرد پرهیزگار = 'into the hand of a certain pious man.' به دست is a prepositional phrase 'into the hand(s) of' (indicating transfer/possession), یکی here is an indefinite 'a certain', followed by مرد 'man' and the adjective پرهیزگار 'pious' modifying مرد.
segments.s169-g247: گسی کن به سوی سیاووش‌گرد = 'Send someone toward Siyâvashgerd.' گسی is an indefinite pronoun 'someone/a certain one', کن is the imperative 'do/make/send' (here governing the implied action of sending), and به سوی + place is the directional prepositional phrase 'toward Siyâvashgerd.'
segments.s169-g248: مگردان بدآموز را هیچ گرد = 'Do not allow the corrupter to go about at all.' مگردان is a negative imperative 'do not turn/allow/let', بدآموز ('ill-instructor' = 'corrupting person') is the object marked by را, and هیچ گرد ('at all' + گرد) expresses complete prevention of his going about; گرد here has the verbal/active sense 'to go about/wander' (i.e. to be at large).
segments.s169-g217: بران is a prepositional particle meaning 'upon/over that' placed before the noun phrase; خسروی modifies یال as an adjective ('regal mane'). The coordinator و links the next noun phrase (آن چنگ او), where آن is a demonstrative 'that' and او is the independent third‑person pronoun indicating possession ('his').
segments.s169-g218: بدان here functions as a compound prepositional unit ('with/and that' or 'to that') introducing another coordinated list: شاخ 'horn' is followed by و and then the demonstrative آن plus فر 'glory' and اورنگ 'throne'. او at the end again marks possession ('his'). The whole phrase continues the enumerative description begun in the previous segment.
segments.s169-g219: زمانی is an adverb meaning 'for a while' that modifies the verbal phrase نگه کرد (the compound 'to hold a gaze' — نگه کردن). The conjunction و links the next clause نیکو بدید where نیکو is an adverb 'well/clearly' and بدید is the past of دیدن 'saw.'
segments.s169-g220: همی is a classical particle here giving immediacy ('then/just so') and combines with the verb گشت 'became/turned' to predicate the state of رنگ رخش ('the colour of Rakhsh'). ناپدید functions as a predicative adjective 'vanished/hidden.' Note the verb precedes the adjectival predicate in this poetic ordering.
segments.s169-g221: تن پهلوان ('the hero's body') is the noun phrase subject; گشت is the past of گشتن 'became' and takes the adjective لرزان 'trembling' as predicate. چو بید is a simile 'like a willow' attached to the predicate to describe the trembling.
segments.s169-g222: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the source phrase 'ز جان جوان پاک' ('from the pure young life'); the verb بگسست ('broke apart/was severed') follows and then the subject امید ('hope') appears — a common classical order where the verb comes before the subject, giving the sense 'hope was severed from his young pure life.'
segments.s169-g223: زمانی 'for a while' plus چنان بود 'it was thus' is a short clause stating condition/state. بگشاد چهر is a verbal phrase 'the face opened' (بگشاد as 'opened' or 'relaxed' and چهر 'face/countenance'), describing a change in appearance following the previous emotion.
segments.s169-g224: زمانه ('fate, the world') is the agent; آورد is past 'brought/caused' and مهر 'love' is the thing brought. The prepositional phrase به دلش اندر ('into his heart') marks the destination ('into his heart'), so the clause means 'fate brought love into his heart.'
segments.s169-g185: This is a temporal/locative phrase: 'ز' is a poetic preposition here ('from/among'), followed by 'پیران' (elders). 'چو' is a temporal conjunction 'when'. The verb 'بشینید' is an archaic past/ perfective form meaning 'sat down' (Ferdowsi often uses بس- / بن- forms where modern Persian uses نشست). The proper name 'افراسیاب' comes at the end as the subject of the clause — classical word order places the verb before the nominal subject.
segments.s169-g186: 'سر مرد جنگی' is a nominal phrase meaning 'the head/of the warlike man' (literally 'the head of the warrior'); Persian often uses 'سرِ X' to mean 'the person X' or 'the chief/leader X' in poetic contexts. The verb 'درآمد' is used intransitively here in the past sense 'arose/came up', and 'ز خواب' is an ablative phrase 'from sleep' indicating source — together conveying 'the warrior rose from sleep' (i.e. awoke).
segments.s169-g187: 'یکی' is an indefinite subject 'one/someone'. 'سخت' here is an adverb 'firmly/strongly' modifying 'سوگند' (oath). In Persian idiom 'سوگند خورد' = 'to take/swear an oath', so 'خورد' is the past of خوردن used idiomatically with 'سوگند' (not literal 'eat'). 'شاهانه' is an adjectival qualifier 'regal/kingly' placed after the noun it modifies, a common poetic order.
segments.s169-g188: This is an adverbial phrase specifying the oath's objects: 'به‌روز' is a prepositional/temporal use 'by the day' (here 'by the bright day'), with the adjective 'سپید' following the noun. The coordinating 'و' links the second half 'شب لاژورد' where 'لاژورد' (lapis/azure) postmodifies 'شب' (night). Such postpositive adjectives and enjambed color imagery are characteristic of Ferdowsi's poetic style.
segments.s169-g189: The clause begins with 'به' used here in the oath-sense 'by' (introducing the sacred referent). 'دادار' = 'the Creator' (vocative/accusative target of the oath). 'کاو' is an archaic relative pronoun equivalent to 'که او' meaning 'who', introducing the relative clause 'who created this world'; 'این جهان' is the direct object 'this world' and 'آفرید' is the past verb 'created' placed at the end in classical order. (Note: 'کاو' is an archaic/formal form of 'که او'.)
segments.s169-g190: This is a paratactic list of the things created: 'سپهر و دد و دام و جان' — 'the sky and (the) enemy and (the) snare and life' — followed by the verb 'آفرید' (created). In Persian poetry the verb may follow a long coordinated series of objects, and the final verb governs the whole list.
segments.s169-g191: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. 'ناید' is an archaic negative modal meaning 'shall not / must not / will not occur' (negative subjunctive/optative). 'بدین' is a contracted prepositional phrase ('به + این') meaning 'to this'; it marks the beneficiary/target 'this child' ('کودک'). 'از من' indicates source/agent 'from me', and 'ستم' is the nominal predicate 'injustice/oppression' — together: 'that no injustice shall come to this child from me.'
segments.s169-g192: This is an emphatic negated continuation. 'نه هرگز' is an emphatic 'no, never'; the sequence 'برو بر زنم تیزدم' is a classical compound of action where 'برو' functions as 'go (and)...' and 'بر' is the preposition 'upon/against' introducing the object of striking. 'زنم' is the 1st-person present/subjunctive of زدن 'to strike/attack', and 'تیزدم' is a final verb/adverbial perfective that intensifies the action — here meaning 'smite sharply' or 'strike swiftly'. The whole means 'I will never go to strike him nor smite him sharply.'
segments.s169-g113: وزان (literally 'and/from that') is an adverbial pointer linking back to the prior clause; جایگه شد is a verbal phrase ('a place was made' / 'appeared') with شد as a past/resultative verb. به + نهاد following it (شیر و پلنگ) marks the reference group ('with respect to / as for the lion and the leopard' or simply 'the lion and the leopard appeared'). The word order is poetic (adverb — predicate — prepositional phrase) but functions like 'from that place there came the lion and the leopard.'
segments.s169-g114: هم is a connector ('also / too') tying this clause to the previous; آن چوب خمیده is a nominal phrase 'that bent stick/staff' (چوب here = stick/staff). بد ساز جنگ is a genitival/attributive phrase literally 'bad-maker-of-war' (ساز = 'maker / means / instrument'); together it means the stick proved an ill instrument for warfare. Adjectival ordering and the compact compound 'بد ساز جنگ' are typical of the poetic diction.
segments.s169-g115: چنین 'thus/so' opens the clause; تا here is temporal 'until'. برآمد is past tense 'arose/issued forth'. برین is the fused/poetic form meaning 'that/aforementioned' (بر + این), so برین روزگار = 'that time/aforementioned age'. The sequence reads 'thus until that time arose'.
segments.s169-g116: بیامد is the past tense of آمدن 'came' (here: 'arrived' or 'came into effect'). به فرمان آموزگار is an adverbial/instrumental phrase 'by the command of the teacher' (به + noun marks agency/instrument here). Word order places the verb before the specifying prepositional phrase, a normal option in Persian narrative.
segments.s169-g117: شبان (plural) is the subject 'shepherds'; اندر آمد is a compound verb 'came in / came' (اندر is a prepositional/adverbial element often used in classical/poetic Persian). ز کوه و ز دشت gives the source ('from the mountain and from the plain'). The order places the motion verb after the subject, with the sources following — typical poetic/narrative ordering.
segments.s169-g118: بنالید is past tense 'they moaned/wailed'; the conjunction و links the following clause. نزدیک پیران is an adverbial phrase 'near the elders' and گذشت is past 'passed (by)'. Subject is omitted in the second clause and is understood to be the same شَبان; the verbs are simple past narrative forms.
segments.s169-g119: که here introduces reported speech/explanation ('for / that'); من is the first‑person subject 'I'. زین in older/poetic Persian can function as زِ این ('from this' / 'because of this') and introduces the circumstance; the lexeme list also records the concrete meaning 'saddle/harness' but here it functions as the fused prepositional 'from/for this'. سرافراز is an epithet 'proud / exalted' qualifying شیر 'lion'; یله ('unrestrained, wild') further qualifies the lion. The whole nominal group zِin + epithets gives 'because of this proud, untamed lion'.
segments.s169-g120: سوی پهلوان آمدم is a straightforward past clause: سوی + noun = 'toward the champion' and آمدم is 1st person past 'I came'. با گله 'with a flock' is an accompanying/instrumental phrase. Word order places the directional phrase before the verb, then the accompaniment after it — a normal narrative pattern.
segments.s169-g313: همی + خواهم forms a habitual/hortative present: 'I keep wishing / I continually ask.' از introduces the source/benefactor ('from'). روشن کردگار is an epithet for God (lit. 'the Luminous/Enlightened Maker'); word order is preposition + epithet + noun.
segments.s169-g314: که introduces a subordinate clause expressing the desired content ('that ...'). چندان زمان is a quantified noun phrase 'so much time.' یابم is first-person singular present-subjunctive/optative of یافتن here meaning 'may I obtain/attain' (it expresses the speaker's wish). از روزگار ('from/following the course of life/fate') is the source phrase linked to یابم.
segments.s169-g315: کزین is a fused/prepositional form meaning 'from this' (akin to 'از این'). نامور نامه باستان is a noun phrase in apposition: 'the famed/renowned ancient book' (name order adjective–noun, archaic/high style).
segments.s169-g316: بمانم is first-person singular present used optatively/jussive ('let there remain / may there remain'); in classical Persian the 1st person is used here to express the speaker's wish that something endure. به گیتی 'in the world' is the locative, and یکی داستان ('one tale') is the logical thing that 'remains' — word order places the predicate before the noun phrase for poetic emphasis.
segments.s169-g317: که here begins a relative/subordinate clause. هرکس که اندر سخن frames 'whoever, in speech,' where اندر ('in') marks the domain (words/poetry). داد داد is a fused noun+verb construction: the first داد functions as 'justice'/'fairness' (noun) and the second داد is the verb 'gave' — together idiomatically meaning 'rendered a fair account / judged rightly / spoke justly' (i.e., 'whoever speaks fairly').
segments.s169-g318: ز من ('concerning/from me') marks the topic. جز به نیکی ('except with goodness/kindness') is an exception phrase. نگیرند is 3rd-person plural present-subjunctive/negative-jussive of گرفتن used here in an optative sense ('may they not take / let them not hold'), and یاد ('remembrance') is the object: 'may they take no remembrance of me except kindness.'
segments.s169-g319: بدان is a high/archaic prepositional expression equivalent to 'به آن' ('in/at that'); here it combines the preposition with a demonstrative. گیتیم is 'my world' (گیتی + possessive -م) so بدین گیتیم = 'in that my world / in that world of mine.' نیز 'also' adds inclusion. خواهشگرست is a present predicate 'is an asker/aspirant/desirous one' — an adjectival/nominal predicate formed with -ست (archaic short form of است). Note: the element دان here does not carry its usual lexical sense 'to know' but functions as part of the fused prepositional 'بدان'.
segments.s169-g320: که introduces a relative clause describing that aspirant. با + noun ('با تیغ') marks accompaniment or the armament ('with a sword'). تیزست is 'is sharp' (تیز + -ست, archaic form of است), so 'with a sharp sword' = 'armed with a sharp sword.' و links the second accompaniment. با افسرست likewise: افسر (helmet) + -ست = 'is (with) helmet' — idiomatically 'and with a helmet' (i.e., wearing a helmet).
segments.s169-g305: همان گوش (literally “that same ear”) is the subject phrase. از آوای او is a prepositional phrase marking the cause/source (“from his voice”). گشت سیر is a copular-like sequence: گشت (past of گشتن, here ‘became’) + predicate adjective سیر (‘sated, full’). Classical word order places the verb after the noun phrases; او is a third‑person possessive pronoun (“his/her”) modifying آوا.
segments.s169-g306: همش (= همه‌اش) “all of it” refers back to the ear’s tone. لحن بلبل is a genitive/attributive phrase “the melody/tone of the nightingale.” هم before آوای شیر functions as a coordinating particle (“and/also”), so آوای شیر is “the voice/cry of the lion.” The segment lists two qualities of the ear’s tone in parallel (soft/nightingale and fierce/lion).
segments.s169-g307: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” برداشتم is first‑person singular past “I lifted / I took up.” جام پنجاه و هشت places the numeral after the noun in classical style and means “the fifty‑eighth cup” (پنجاه و هشت = 58). The clause sets the time/scene for what follows.
segments.s169-g308: نگیرم is the first‑person verb form (root گرفتن) used here to indicate that the speaker ‘took’ or ‘held’ nothing — classically the negative prefix ن can produce a result sense in narrative, so the whole means “I took nothing.” به جز = “except.” یاد تابوت و تشت is the direct object of that exclusion: “the memory/remembrance of the coffin and the basin.” The copular/tense sense is elliptical and classical Persian often compresses the tense/aspect in this way.
segments.s169-g309: دریغ is an interjection “pity / alas.” آن introduces the enumerated nouns that follow (گل و مشک و خوشاب = “flower, musk, and sweet draught/wine”) and forms a nominal exclamatory phrase. This segment is a nominal lament; the predicate that connects or explains it appears in the next segment (ellipsis of a copula is normal in classical Persian).
segments.s169-g310: همان is a demonstrative emphatic “that very / the same.” تیغ is the noun “sword,” برنده is an adjective/participle meaning “sharp, cutting,” and پارسی is an attributive adjective “Persian.” There is no overt copula; the phrase serves as the (understood) cause/agent or identification that completes the lament in the previous segment: “(it was) that very sharp Persian sword.”
segments.s169-g311: نگردد is the negative non‑past/impersonal form of گشتن/گردیدن here meaning “will not turn / will not go round.” همی is an emphatic adverb (“anymore / now no longer / still” depending on context). گرد in this line is a noun “dust” (not the preposition), and نسرین is the possessor (“the dust of the nesrīn/wild‑rose”). تذرو is a verb “to scatter/disperse”; together the clause says that the nesrin’s dust will no longer be scattered (i.e., the petals/dust will not whirl any more) — classical Persian often strings nouns and verbs with ellipsis and poetic compression.
segments.s169-g312: گل نارون is a genitive phrase “the flower/blossom of the elm.” خواهد is the future/auxiliary sense of خواستن here (“will [do] / will long for / will desire”); in poetic contexts it can indicate what will happen next or what will be felt. و connects the final noun phrase شاخ سرو (“the cypress bough”), where the verb is again omitted by ellipsis: the line lists natural things and states that they will be affected (yearn, grieve, etc.).
